source.title: End-to-End SLI Composition and Cross-Service Correlation Playbook
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Summary:
This playbook defines how Redwood composes end-to-end Service Level Indicators (SLIs) for multi-stage inference pipelines and how teams should correlate SLIs with distributed traces and logs to detect regressions and root cause performance issues quickly. The guidance targets hosted, dedicated, and private deployments where requests traverse the API gateway, routing layer, serving runtime, and optional post-processing services.

Goals:
- Provide unambiguous SLI definitions suitable for alerting and SLA reporting.
- Ensure SLIs are computable from existing telemetry within cost and retention constraints.
- Standardize correlation keys and span/metric tags so incidents can be traced end-to-end.
- Offer pragmatic aggregation, bucketing, and sampling rules to balance observability and ingestion cost.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: API request paths that produce or route tokens (generation, chat, embeddings, reranking) and their synchronous downstream processing. Includes cache/KV warmup effects, autoscaling boundaries, and model fallback events.
- Non-goal: Offline batch ML training or ad-hoc evaluation runs (refer to applied-ml-and-evals docs).

Key definitions (terms used below):
- request_id: unique UUID per user-facing request at the API boundary; must be present in logs, metrics, and top-level trace.
- stage: logical pipeline segment (api_gateway, router, runtime, postprocess, cache).
- success: an HTTP 2xx at the API boundary and absence of downstream 'response_error' spans.
- eligible_request: request that should be counted for an SLI (see sampling rules).

SLI catalogue and formulas:
1) P50/P95/P99 end-to-end latency (user-perceived, in ms)
- Definition: latency_end_to_end = time(api_gateway.request_received) -> time(api_gateway.response_sent) for eligible_request.
- Computation: compute histogram of request latencies; report percentiles over a 5-minute sliding window and 1-hour compliance window.
- Alerts: p99 > 1200ms for 5m => page. p95 > 600ms for 10m => pager depending on SLO tier.

2) Token emission latency (per-token service latency)
- Definition: token_latency = time(token_emit_span.start) -> time(token_emit_span.end) within runtime stage; tag with model_id and sequence_length_bucket.
- Use-case: uncovers token-level stalls due to KV cache misses or kernel scheduling.

3) Successful-generation rate (quality-preserving availability)
- Definition: success_rate = successful_responses / eligible_requests over a 1h window; success includes response schema validation and no downstream function error.
- Alert: success_rate < 99.5% for customer-facing hosted tier => investigate.

4) Cache effectiveness SLI (runtime)
- Definition: cache_hit_ratio = cache_hits / cache_lookups observed by runtime for generation requests.
- Expose: runtime emits metric runtime.cache.hits and runtime.cache.lookups with stage=runtime and model_id.
- Use: informs batching/kv_warmup regressions.

Aggregation rules and windows:
- Primary reporting windows: 5m (fast detection), 1h (operational), 24h (trend).
- Percentiles: use HDR histograms at ingestion for accurate high-percentile reporting. Avoid reconstructing percentiles from summaries if original histogram is available.
- Bucketing: always compute latency histograms broken down by model_id, region, and traffic_class (internal/customer/dedicated).

Sampling and cost controls:
- Sampling policy: By default, sample 100% of requests that are classified as high-priority (traffic_class=customer or SLA-bound). For free-tier or exploratory customers, sample 10% with reservoir sampling to preserve burst coverage.
- Trace sampling: retain 100% of traces for SLI-failing requests (response status >=500 or p95>threshold) and a warm reservoir of 0.5% of successful requests per minute.
- Metric cardinality guardrails: model_id and route are allowed high-cardinality tags; do not create per- prompt-id or per-user tags at high ingestion rates. Use hashed_user_cohort instead.

Correlation keys and span/metric tag conventions (required):
- request_id (string) - top-level required across logs, metrics, traces.
- request.origin (api | sdk | internal) - route-level tag for traffic segmentation.
- model_id (string) - canonical model identifier used by routing layer.
- routing_decision (primary | fallback | degraded-quant) - mark when fallback occurred.
- sequence_length_bucket (short) - small enum like <=64, 65-256, 257-1024, >1024.
- token_index (int) - used only in token-emission spans when sampling allows.

Tracing guidance (spans and parent/child rules):
- Top-level span: api_gateway.request (contains request_id and origin).
- Router span: router.decision (includes routing_decision, model_id, route_latency_ms).
- Runtime spans: runtime.infer (split into runtime.kv_fetch, runtime.kernel_exec, runtime.kv_commit).
- Postprocess spans: postprocess.rule_eval, postprocess.formatting.
- Add logs as events on spans instead of separate free-form logs where possible (cut down correlation work).

Example span tags (JSON example):
- {
    "span.name": "runtime.kv_fetch",
    "request_id": "<uuid>",
    "model_id": "redwood/gpt-lite-8k",
    "sequence_length_bucket": "65-256",
    "routing_decision": "primary"
  }

PromQL / query examples (Datadog/Prometheus style):
- End-to-end p95 (histogram): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(api_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, model_id, region))
- Cache hit ratio (ratio): sum(rate(runtime_cache_hits_total[5m])) by (model_id) / sum(rate(runtime_cache_lookups_total[5m])) by (model_id)

Alerting and on-call playbook (short):
1) Alert fires (p99 latency) -> paging group: inference-slo-ops.
2) On-call engineer: check primary dashboard (see Dashboard links) and recent traces filtered by request_id.
3) If routing_decision=fallback increased in last 10m, escalate to runtime-oncall. If kernel_exec time is high for a single model_id, create a model-performance ticket.
4) If cache_hit_ratio dropped >20% for model_id and coincides with spike, trigger investigation into KV partitioning and prewarming policies.

Dashboards and observability surfaces (required):
- SLI Overview: p50/p95/p99 latency, success_rate, requests_per_min, error_rate (broken down by region and model_id).
- Runtime Health: kernel_exec P95, kv_fetch latency, GPU_utilization, cache_hit_ratio.
- Routing & Fallbacks: routing_decision counts over time, fallback_reason breakdown.
- Traces: top 100 slow traces sampled over 1h with span waterfall and tag filters.

Rollout checklist for SLI-aware changes (PR/Release stage):
1) Add or update SLI metrics and spike-tested histograms in a feature branch.
2) Add unit tests for any new tag names and backwards-compatible parsing.
3) Deploy with 0.1% traffic shadow and verify that tags and spans appear.
4) Enable 5% traffic, validate dashboards and synthetic transactions.
5) Gradual rollout with monitoring for 72 hours; if p95 regresses >10% relative to baseline, automatic rollback.

Instrumenting legacy services (practical steps):
- If adding request_id is impossible at source, inject at the first proxy and add span.event:request_id_injected=true. Mark these traces in dashboards so they are distinguishable.
- For services lacking histograms, emit latency summary and a small set of latency buckets (50, 100, 250, 500, 1000, 2000 ms).

Ownership and SLAs:
- SLI owner: inference-slo-ops (primary), eng-platform (secondary).
- SLA reporting cadence: automated weekly report for sales and account teams; monthly executive summary.

Related artifacts and links:
- Telemetry ingestion & retention guidelines (telemetry-ingestion-and-retention-guidelines-2025)
- ADR: Standard metadata envelope (adr-standard-metadata-envelope-and-hierarchical-throttling-2025)

Change log:
- 2025-07-10: Initial draft and internal review.
- 2025-11-05: Added token-level SLI and cache effectiveness metrics after runtime perf incidents.
- 2026-01-18: Updated sampling policy and alert thresholds; aligned with Dedicated tier SLOs.

Appendix: quick checklist for engineers instrumenting an endpoint:
- Ensure request_id present and propagated.
- Emit histograms at API boundary and runtime boundary.
- Tag spans with model_id and routing_decision.
- Add metric for cache.lookups and cache.hits.
- Verify dashboards and run synthetic 1k request throughput test.

Owners and contacts:
- Primary: inference-slo-ops@redwood.internal
- Eng platform lead: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.internal)

This playbook is intended to be pragmatic: measure what you can reliably compute and correlate quickly. If you need help mapping an internal service to these conventions, contact inference-slo-ops for an instrumentation review.

Goals:
- Provide unambiguous SLI definitions suitable for alerting and SLA reporting.
- Ensure SLIs are computable from existing telemetry within cost and retention constraints.
- Standardize correlation keys and span/metric tags so incidents can be traced end-to-end.
- Offer pragmatic aggregation, bucketing, and sampling rules to balance observability and ingestion cost.

Scope and non-goals:
- Scope: API request paths that produce or route tokens (generation, chat, embeddings, reranking) and their synchronous downstream processing. Includes cache/KV warmup effects, autoscaling boundaries, and model fallback events.
- Non-goal: Offline batch ML training or ad-hoc evaluation runs (refer to applied-ml-and-evals docs).

Key definitions (terms used below):
- request_id: unique UUID per user-facing request at the API boundary; must be present in logs, metrics, and top-level trace.
- stage: logical pipeline segment (api_gateway, router, runtime, postprocess, cache).
- success: an HTTP 2xx at the API boundary and absence of downstream 'response_error' spans.
- eligible_request: request that should be counted for an SLI (see sampling rules).

SLI catalogue and formulas:
1) P50/P95/P99 end-to-end latency (user-perceived, in ms)
- Definition: latency_end_to_end = time(api_gateway.request_received) -> time(api_gateway.response_sent) for eligible_request.
- Computation: compute histogram of request latencies; report percentiles over a 5-minute sliding window and 1-hour compliance window.
- Alerts: p99 > 1200ms for 5m => page. p95 > 600ms for 10m => pager depending on SLO tier.

2) Token emission latency (per-token service latency)
- Definition: token_latency = time(token_emit_span.start) -> time(token_emit_span.end) within runtime stage; tag with model_id and sequence_length_bucket.
- Use-case: uncovers token-level stalls due to KV cache misses or kernel scheduling.

3) Successful-generation rate (quality-preserving availability)
- Definition: success_rate = successful_responses / eligible_requests over a 1h window; success includes response schema validation and no downstream function error.
- Alert: success_rate < 99.5% for customer-facing hosted tier => investigate.

4) Cache effectiveness SLI (runtime)
- Definition: cache_hit_ratio = cache_hits / cache_lookups observed by runtime for generation requests.
- Expose: runtime emits metric runtime.cache.hits and runtime.cache.lookups with stage=runtime and model_id.
- Use: informs batching/kv_warmup regressions.

Aggregation rules and windows:
- Primary reporting windows: 5m (fast detection), 1h (operational), 24h (trend).
- Percentiles: use HDR histograms at ingestion for accurate high-percentile reporting. Avoid reconstructing percentiles from summaries if original histogram is available.
- Bucketing: always compute latency histograms broken down by model_id, region, and traffic_class (internal/customer/dedicated).

Sampling and cost controls:
- Sampling policy: By default, sample 100% of requests that are classified as high-priority (traffic_class=customer or SLA-bound). For free-tier or exploratory customers, sample 10% with reservoir sampling to preserve burst coverage.
- Trace sampling: retain 100% of traces for SLI-failing requests (response status >=500 or p95>threshold) and a warm reservoir of 0.5% of successful requests per minute.
- Metric cardinality guardrails: model_id and route are allowed high-cardinality tags; do not create per- prompt-id or per-user tags at high ingestion rates. Use hashed_user_cohort instead.

Correlation keys and span/metric tag conventions (required):
- request_id (string) - top-level required across logs, metrics, traces.
- request.origin (api | sdk | internal) - route-level tag for traffic segmentation.
- model_id (string) - canonical model identifier used by routing layer.
- routing_decision (primary | fallback | degraded-quant) - mark when fallback occurred.
- sequence_length_bucket (short) - small enum like <=64, 65-256, 257-1024, >1024.
- token_index (int) - used only in token-emission spans when sampling allows.

Tracing guidance (spans and parent/child rules):
- Top-level span: api_gateway.request (contains request_id and origin).
- Router span: router.decision (includes routing_decision, model_id, route_latency_ms).
- Runtime spans: runtime.infer (split into runtime.kv_fetch, runtime.kernel_exec, runtime.kv_commit).
- Postprocess spans: postprocess.rule_eval, postprocess.formatting.
- Add logs as events on spans instead of separate free-form logs where possible (cut down correlation work).

Example span tags (JSON example):
- {
    "span.name": "runtime.kv_fetch",
    "request_id": "<uuid>",
    "model_id": "redwood/gpt-lite-8k",
    "sequence_length_bucket": "65-256",
    "routing_decision": "primary"
  }

PromQL / query examples (Datadog/Prometheus style):
- End-to-end p95 (histogram): histogram_quantile(0.95, sum(rate(api_gateway_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le, model_id, region))
- Cache hit ratio (ratio): sum(rate(runtime_cache_hits_total[5m])) by (model_id) / sum(rate(runtime_cache_lookups_total[5m])) by (model_id)

Alerting and on-call playbook (short):
1) Alert fires (p99 latency) -> paging group: inference-slo-ops.
2) On-call engineer: check primary dashboard (see Dashboard links) and recent traces filtered by request_id.
3) If routing_decision=fallback increased in last 10m, escalate to runtime-oncall. If kernel_exec time is high for a single model_id, create a model-performance ticket.
4) If cache_hit_ratio dropped >20% for model_id and coincides with spike, trigger investigation into KV partitioning and prewarming policies.

Dashboards and observability surfaces (required):
- SLI Overview: p50/p95/p99 latency, success_rate, requests_per_min, error_rate (broken down by region and model_id).
- Runtime Health: kernel_exec P95, kv_fetch latency, GPU_utilization, cache_hit_ratio.
- Routing & Fallbacks: routing_decision counts over time, fallback_reason breakdown.
- Traces: top 100 slow traces sampled over 1h with span waterfall and tag filters.

Rollout checklist for SLI-aware changes (PR/Release stage):
1) Add or update SLI metrics and spike-tested histograms in a feature branch.
2) Add unit tests for any new tag names and backwards-compatible parsing.
3) Deploy with 0.1% traffic shadow and verify that tags and spans appear.
4) Enable 5% traffic, validate dashboards and synthetic transactions.
5) Gradual rollout with monitoring for 72 hours; if p95 regresses >10% relative to baseline, automatic rollback.

Instrumenting legacy services (practical steps):
- If adding request_id is impossible at source, inject at the first proxy and add span.event:request_id_injected=true. Mark these traces in dashboards so they are distinguishable.
- For services lacking histograms, emit latency summary and a small set of latency buckets (50, 100, 250, 500, 1000, 2000 ms).

Ownership and SLAs:
- SLI owner: inference-slo-ops (primary), eng-platform (secondary).
- SLA reporting cadence: automated weekly report for sales and account teams; monthly executive summary.

Related artifacts and links:
- Telemetry ingestion & retention guidelines (telemetry-ingestion-and-retention-guidelines-2025)
- ADR: Standard metadata envelope (adr-standard-metadata-envelope-and-hierarchical-throttling-2025)

Change log:
- 2025-07-10: Initial draft and internal review.
- 2025-11-05: Added token-level SLI and cache effectiveness metrics after runtime perf incidents.
- 2026-01-18: Updated sampling policy and alert thresholds; aligned with Dedicated tier SLOs.

Appendix: quick checklist for engineers instrumenting an endpoint:
- Ensure request_id present and propagated.
- Emit histograms at API boundary and runtime boundary.
- Tag spans with model_id and routing_decision.
- Add metric for cache.lookups and cache.hits.
- Verify dashboards and run synthetic 1k request throughput test.

Owners and contacts:
- Primary: inference-slo-ops@redwood.internal
- Eng platform lead: Priya Natarajan (priya@redwood.internal)

This playbook is intended to be pragmatic: measure what you can reliably compute and correlate quickly. If you need help mapping an internal service to these conventions, contact inference-slo-ops for an instrumentation review.

source.title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-01-16)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
# Summary
Orion is progressing, but we have an emerging integration risk with the runtime router path for fallback decisions.

# Status
- Overall: At Risk
- RAG: Red

# Progress this week
- Policy engine skeleton merged behind a feature flag (orion_policy_engine_enabled).
- Defined fallback precedence rules for model variants (pinning > compatibility > cost).

# Risks / blockers
- Runtime router currently applies fallback in a different code path than the control plane expects; we may need a refactor or a shim layer.
- Timeline risk: if refactor is required, console beta slips by 1–2 weeks.

# Mitigations
- Schedule a 90-minute design review with runtime + platform owners.
- Implement a temporary adapter that maps router decisions into Orion policy evaluation events.

# Next week
- Prototype adapter in staging and validate event schema.

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Connor OBrien (runtime routing), Maya Srinivasan (console)

source.title: Project Orion — Weekly status update (2026-01-09)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
# Summary
Project Orion is the cross-team effort to unify rollout controls (canary, A/B, and automatic fallback) into a single policy surface in the Redwood Console and routing layer.

# Status
- Overall: On Track
- RAG: Green

# Progress this week
- Landed initial API contract for policy evaluation (route selector -> policy engine).
- Console wireframes approved for the policy editor v1.
- Defined success criteria for v0 (single-model canary + fallback).

# Risks / blockers
- None material.

# Decisions
- Orion v0 will support canary by percentage and automatic rollback on 5xx rate regression.

# Next week
- Implement policy engine skeleton in platform-control-plane.
- Start integration with runtime router (read-only evaluation).

# Owners
- PM: Ava Chen
- Eng: Alex Martinez (platform), Maya Srinivasan (console)

source.title: Status page + customer comms templates (failover / regional outage)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
h1. Purpose
Provide consistent external communications during outages and failovers. These templates are written to be accurate even when details are still emerging.

h1. Principles
- Acknowledge impact and symptoms (errors/latency).
- Avoid speculation about root cause.
- State what we are doing next (investigating, mitigating, failing over).
- Provide the next update time.

h1. Status page template: incident start
*Title:* Increased error rates for Redwood Hosted API
*Body:* We are investigating elevated error rates and latency affecting a subset of requests to the Redwood Hosted API. We have identified a regional service degradation and are working to mitigate. Next update in 30 minutes.

h1. Status page template: failover initiated
*Title:* Regional degradation; traffic failover in progress
*Body:* We are performing a controlled traffic failover to a secondary region to mitigate ongoing elevated error rates. During this process, some requests may see transient errors or increased latency. Next update in 30 minutes.

h1. Status page template: failover complete
*Title:* Traffic routed to secondary region
*Body:* We have completed traffic failover to a secondary region and are monitoring service stability. Some users may continue to experience elevated latency while capacity stabilizes. Next update in 60 minutes.

h1. Status page template: resolved
*Title:* Resolved: Hosted API errors and latency
*Body:* This incident is resolved. We will publish a follow-up with additional details after internal review.

h1. Direct customer email template (enterprise)
*Subject:* Update: Redwood Hosted API regional incident
*Body:* Hello,

We are currently mitigating a regional incident impacting the Redwood Hosted API. To restore service, we are failing over traffic to a secondary region. During failover, you may observe intermittent errors or increased latency.

We will provide the next update by <TIME>. If you have urgent questions, reply to this email and our on-call team will respond.

Regards,
Redwood Inference Support

h1. Support handoff notes
- Comms lead should post the chosen template in the incident channel for IC approval before publishing.
- If specific customers are heavily impacted, Support should reference their observed error rates from dashboards (avoid internal metric names in external emails).

source.title: Operator Safety Net: Simulation & Training Playbook
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministic, hands-on safety net for exercising runbooks before incidents occur and to validate dashboard alerting/automations end-to-end.

Why this exists:

- Many incidents are caused by subtle multi-signal interactions that are hard to reproduce in production.
- Operators need repeatable, time-boxed exercises so oncall fatigue and cognitive load can be reduced.
- Verifying dashboards, runbooks, and automation together prevents false confidence in isolated tests.

Scope and non-goals:

- In scope: platform-level degradations that affect inference latency and token-cost SLOs for multi-tenant workloads. Includes KV prefix cache saturation, batching amplification, model-variant fallback storms, and GPU interference scenarios.
- Out of scope: application-layer bugs inside customer code, and multi-region network partitions (handled in a separate DR runbook).

Owners and stakeholders:

- Primary owner: Platform Operations (eng-platform).
- Secondary owners: Serving Runtime, SRE, Developer Experience.
- Stakeholders: Customer Success (for critical tenant scenarios), Product (for SLO changes).

SLOs and success criteria for exercises:

1) Exercise completion criteria: runbook executed within target RTO and operator checklist marked complete.
2) Detection fidelity: dashboards and alerts must surface the primary root-cause signal within 120s of injection 90% of runs.
3) Mitigation effectiveness: automated fallback or manual operator action returns 75th percentile latency within 2x baseline within 15 minutes.

Simulation design overview:

- Architecture: lightweight simulation harness runs in a sandboxed k8s namespace. Each scenario composes workload drivers (request generators), resource signals (GPU stress pods, fio-style disk I/O), and state mutators (cache eviction script, model-swap controller).
- Orchestration: a central experiment controller (small CLI) deploys scenario manifests, advances phases, and records timestamps for key events. The harness replays recorded sequences with deterministic timings to ensure reproducibility.
- Observability hooks: every run attaches a trace id prefixed with sim-run/<run-id> and emits a run manifest to the run-archive S3 bucket for postmortem analysis.

Key scenarios (templates):

1) KV Prefix Cache Saturation
- Trigger: burst of synthetic prefix-heavy traffic that exceeds cache capacity by 3x for 10 minutes.
- Expected signals: KV cache hit-rate drop, token-per-request increase, CPU throttle on cache sharders.
- Operator actions: throttle offending callers, clear high-entropy prefixes, trigger cache-resizing or manual shard rebalance.

2) Batching Amplification Spike
- Trigger: sudden concentration of large-sequence requests from a tenant causing batching queue length growth and tail latency amplification.
- Expected signals: queue length > 95th percentile, token latency tail rises, autoscaler lag.
- Operator actions: apply soft-quotas, enable tenant burst-credit, temporarily pin to smaller model-variant.

3) Model-Variant Fallback Storm
- Trigger: upstream model registry rollout misconfig leads to many requests falling back to slower CPU-quantized variants.
- Expected signals: model-swap events, cross-service error-rate changes, cost-per-token increase.
- Operator actions: rollback rollout, enact emergency model-swap runbook, throttle non-critical routes.

4) GPU Interference and Preemption Mix
- Trigger: scheduled maintenance + spot preemption simulation to force resource balancing events across tenant pools.
- Expected signals: GPU queue stalls, eviction events, tenant throughput loss.
- Operator actions: activate burst-pools, migrate critical tenants to reserved capacity, escalate to capacity-ops.

Runner blueprint (manifest fields):

| field | description | example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| name | scenario id | kv-prefix-saturation-202511 |
| duration | planned wallclock | 00:20:00 |
| traffic_profile | request generator name | prefix-heavy-replay-v2 |
| resource_stress | list of stressors | gpu-stress:2, fio-iops:1000 |
| observability_tag | trace tag prefix | sim-run/kv-202511 |
| operator_checklist | runbook ids to exercise | RB-011, RB-023 |

Deployment and sandboxing steps (operator checklist):

1) Create sandbox namespace: platform-sim-<run-id>.
2) Apply scenario manifest with simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>.
3) Verify observability pipeline has registered sim-run/<run-id> traces.
4) Wait for warmup period (default 2 minutes) then advance to injection phase with simctl inject --phase=1.
5) Monitor dashboards; if auto-safeguard trips, document at what timestamp it activated.
6) Run mitigation steps from the linked runbook.
7) Tear down with simctl teardown --force and collect artifacts.

Dashboards and alerting required for meaningful runs:

- Simulation overview dashboard (templated): shows active runs, phase, run-id, last-heartbeat.
- Multi-signal correlation dashboard: KV cache hit-rate, batch queue depth, per-tenant token latency, GPU utilization, model-swap rates.
- Alerts to validate: 
  - sim:primary-detection (SLO: fire when cache hit-rate drops > 15% and queue depth > threshold for 60s)
  - sim:cost-burn (SLO: fire when cost-per-token > 2x baseline for 5m)
  - sim:auto-safeguard (non-actionable alert used to validate automation trigger)

Example alert rule (pseudo-YAML):

rule: sim:primary-detection
expr: (kv_hit_rate{env="sim"} < 0.65 and batch_queue_depth > 200)
for: 1m
labels:
  severity: critical
annotations:
  runbook: RB-011
  description: "Primary detection for simulated KV cache saturation ({{ $labels.run_id }})"

Operator training curriculum (4-week rotation template):

Week 0: Prep and onboarding
- Read this playbook and run the dry-run tutorial.
- Complete the simulator quickstart (local mode).

Week 1: Detection and Triage drills
- Exercises: KV saturation, batching spike.
- Goals: surface triage steps, dashboard readouts, and signal correlation.

Week 2: Mitigation choreography
- Exercises: model fallback rollback, cache-resize.
- Goals: complete manual mitigation and practice automation handovers.

Week 3: Automation and Postmortem
- Exercises: enable/disable auto-safeguards, run retrospective runbook.
- Goals: confirm run-archive completeness, artifact retrieval, and postmortem write-up.

Assessment rubric (pass/fail and scoring):

- Detection time (40%): <120s = full points, 120–300s = partial, >300s = fail.
- Correct mitigation (40%): all required actions performed in sequence without missed steps.
- Documentation quality (20%): clear artifacts, run-archive links, and lessons.

Runbook integration checklist (for runbook owners):

- Link runbook IDs used in scenario manifest to runbook pages.
- Ensure runbook has an operator checklist with explicit CLI/console commands and expected telemetry.
- Add a short preface "simulation-tested" with the last test date.

Post-run retrospective template (to be completed within 48h):

1) Run summary: short description of scenario and run id.
2) Timeline: injection time, detection time, mitigation start, mitigation end.
3) Gaps found: dashboards that missed signals, flapping automations, noise.
4) Action items: owner, ETA, risk, and acceptance criteria.

Retention and artifacts:

- Artifacts stored in s3://platform-sim-archive/<year>/<run-id> including: run-manifest.json, traces (jaeger), raw logs (gz), benchmark CSVs.
- Retention policy: 365 days by default; critical-run artifacts marked for 3-year retention.

Safety and guardrails:

- Sandbox network egress disabled by default.
- All synthetic traffic is tagged and rate-limited; traffic generators include kill-switch endpoints.
- Only operators in group platform-sim-admins can run destructive scenarios.

Oncall play path (how to escalate during live production discoveries triggered by simulation):

1) If a simulation accidentally impacts prod (unlikely but possible): immediately run simctl teardown --force and notify SRE incident channel.
2) Follow the Production Impact Emergency Checklist (linked RB-900).

Implementation roadmap (next 12 months):

- Q1 2025: ship simctl v0.1, create three canonical scenario manifests, build the simulation overview dashboard.
- Q2 2025: integrate sim-run tags into trace sampler, hook run-archive retention policy.
- Q3 2025: operator training pilot across two oncall rotations. Measure detection fidelity.
- Q4 2025: automate basic mitigations and gate auto-safeguard playbook behind runbook approval.

Metrics to track for program health:

- simulation_runs_per_month
- detection_time_p50_p95
- percent_runs_with_complete_artifacts
- operator_pass_rate_after-training

Appendices:

A) Minimal sim manifest example:

---
name: kv-prefix-saturation-example
duration: 00:15:00
traffic_profile: prefix-heavy-replay-v2
resource_stress: [gpu-stress:1]
observability_tag: sim-run/kv-example
operator_checklist: [RB-011]
---

B) Common CLI commands:

- simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>    # deploys and schedules run
- simctl inject --run <run-id> --phase=1   # advance to injection
- simctl status --run <run-id>   # show run state and phases
- simctl teardown --run <run-id> --force   # immediate cleanup

C) Contacts and escalation paths:

- Platform Operations Pager: #platform-ops (Slack)
- Serving Runtime lead: Diego Morales (diego@redwood.ai)
- SRE oncall: see rota page /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotations

Revision history:

- 2025-02-14: Draft created by Ava Chen.
- 2025-05-08: Added GPU interference scenario and sample alert rule. (Reviewer: Priya Nair)
- 2025-11-09: Polished operator curriculum and retention policy. (Reviewer: Liam O'Sullivan)

Summary:

This playbook establishes a reproducible simulation framework and an operator training curriculum for proactive detection, mitigation, and escalation during multi-signal platform degradations (cache churn, batch spikes, GPU contention, and model fallback churn). The goal is to give operators a deterministic, hands-on safety net for exercising runbooks before incidents occur and to validate dashboard alerting/automations end-to-end.

Why this exists:

- Many incidents are caused by subtle multi-signal interactions that are hard to reproduce in production.
- Operators need repeatable, time-boxed exercises so oncall fatigue and cognitive load can be reduced.
- Verifying dashboards, runbooks, and automation together prevents false confidence in isolated tests.

Scope and non-goals:

- In scope: platform-level degradations that affect inference latency and token-cost SLOs for multi-tenant workloads. Includes KV prefix cache saturation, batching amplification, model-variant fallback storms, and GPU interference scenarios.
- Out of scope: application-layer bugs inside customer code, and multi-region network partitions (handled in a separate DR runbook).

Owners and stakeholders:

- Primary owner: Platform Operations (eng-platform).
- Secondary owners: Serving Runtime, SRE, Developer Experience.
- Stakeholders: Customer Success (for critical tenant scenarios), Product (for SLO changes).

SLOs and success criteria for exercises:

1) Exercise completion criteria: runbook executed within target RTO and operator checklist marked complete.
2) Detection fidelity: dashboards and alerts must surface the primary root-cause signal within 120s of injection 90% of runs.
3) Mitigation effectiveness: automated fallback or manual operator action returns 75th percentile latency within 2x baseline within 15 minutes.

Simulation design overview:

- Architecture: lightweight simulation harness runs in a sandboxed k8s namespace. Each scenario composes workload drivers (request generators), resource signals (GPU stress pods, fio-style disk I/O), and state mutators (cache eviction script, model-swap controller).
- Orchestration: a central experiment controller (small CLI) deploys scenario manifests, advances phases, and records timestamps for key events. The harness replays recorded sequences with deterministic timings to ensure reproducibility.
- Observability hooks: every run attaches a trace id prefixed with sim-run/<run-id> and emits a run manifest to the run-archive S3 bucket for postmortem analysis.

Key scenarios (templates):

1) KV Prefix Cache Saturation
- Trigger: burst of synthetic prefix-heavy traffic that exceeds cache capacity by 3x for 10 minutes.
- Expected signals: KV cache hit-rate drop, token-per-request increase, CPU throttle on cache sharders.
- Operator actions: throttle offending callers, clear high-entropy prefixes, trigger cache-resizing or manual shard rebalance.

2) Batching Amplification Spike
- Trigger: sudden concentration of large-sequence requests from a tenant causing batching queue length growth and tail latency amplification.
- Expected signals: queue length > 95th percentile, token latency tail rises, autoscaler lag.
- Operator actions: apply soft-quotas, enable tenant burst-credit, temporarily pin to smaller model-variant.

3) Model-Variant Fallback Storm
- Trigger: upstream model registry rollout misconfig leads to many requests falling back to slower CPU-quantized variants.
- Expected signals: model-swap events, cross-service error-rate changes, cost-per-token increase.
- Operator actions: rollback rollout, enact emergency model-swap runbook, throttle non-critical routes.

4) GPU Interference and Preemption Mix
- Trigger: scheduled maintenance + spot preemption simulation to force resource balancing events across tenant pools.
- Expected signals: GPU queue stalls, eviction events, tenant throughput loss.
- Operator actions: activate burst-pools, migrate critical tenants to reserved capacity, escalate to capacity-ops.

Runner blueprint (manifest fields):

| field | description | example |
| --- | --- | --- |
| name | scenario id | kv-prefix-saturation-202511 |
| duration | planned wallclock | 00:20:00 |
| traffic_profile | request generator name | prefix-heavy-replay-v2 |
| resource_stress | list of stressors | gpu-stress:2, fio-iops:1000 |
| observability_tag | trace tag prefix | sim-run/kv-202511 |
| operator_checklist | runbook ids to exercise | RB-011, RB-023 |

Deployment and sandboxing steps (operator checklist):

1) Create sandbox namespace: platform-sim-<run-id>.
2) Apply scenario manifest with simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>.
3) Verify observability pipeline has registered sim-run/<run-id> traces.
4) Wait for warmup period (default 2 minutes) then advance to injection phase with simctl inject --phase=1.
5) Monitor dashboards; if auto-safeguard trips, document at what timestamp it activated.
6) Run mitigation steps from the linked runbook.
7) Tear down with simctl teardown --force and collect artifacts.

Dashboards and alerting required for meaningful runs:

- Simulation overview dashboard (templated): shows active runs, phase, run-id, last-heartbeat.
- Multi-signal correlation dashboard: KV cache hit-rate, batch queue depth, per-tenant token latency, GPU utilization, model-swap rates.
- Alerts to validate: 
  - sim:primary-detection (SLO: fire when cache hit-rate drops > 15% and queue depth > threshold for 60s)
  - sim:cost-burn (SLO: fire when cost-per-token > 2x baseline for 5m)
  - sim:auto-safeguard (non-actionable alert used to validate automation trigger)

Example alert rule (pseudo-YAML):

rule: sim:primary-detection
expr: (kv_hit_rate{env="sim"} < 0.65 and batch_queue_depth > 200)
for: 1m
labels:
  severity: critical
annotations:
  runbook: RB-011
  description: "Primary detection for simulated KV cache saturation ({{ $labels.run_id }})"

Operator training curriculum (4-week rotation template):

Week 0: Prep and onboarding
- Read this playbook and run the dry-run tutorial.
- Complete the simulator quickstart (local mode).

Week 1: Detection and Triage drills
- Exercises: KV saturation, batching spike.
- Goals: surface triage steps, dashboard readouts, and signal correlation.

Week 2: Mitigation choreography
- Exercises: model fallback rollback, cache-resize.
- Goals: complete manual mitigation and practice automation handovers.

Week 3: Automation and Postmortem
- Exercises: enable/disable auto-safeguards, run retrospective runbook.
- Goals: confirm run-archive completeness, artifact retrieval, and postmortem write-up.

Assessment rubric (pass/fail and scoring):

- Detection time (40%): <120s = full points, 120–300s = partial, >300s = fail.
- Correct mitigation (40%): all required actions performed in sequence without missed steps.
- Documentation quality (20%): clear artifacts, run-archive links, and lessons.

Runbook integration checklist (for runbook owners):

- Link runbook IDs used in scenario manifest to runbook pages.
- Ensure runbook has an operator checklist with explicit CLI/console commands and expected telemetry.
- Add a short preface \"simulation-tested\" with the last test date.

Post-run retrospective template (to be completed within 48h):

1) Run summary: short description of scenario and run id.
2) Timeline: injection time, detection time, mitigation start, mitigation end.
3) Gaps found: dashboards that missed signals, flapping automations, noise.
4) Action items: owner, ETA, risk, and acceptance criteria.

Retention and artifacts:

- Artifacts stored in s3://platform-sim-archive/<year>/<run-id> including: run-manifest.json, traces (jaeger), raw logs (gz), benchmark CSVs.
- Retention policy: 365 days by default; critical-run artifacts marked for 3-year retention.

Safety and guardrails:

- Sandbox network egress disabled by default.
- All synthetic traffic is tagged and rate-limited; traffic generators include kill-switch endpoints.
- Only operators in group platform-sim-admins can run destructive scenarios.

Oncall play path (how to escalate during live production discoveries triggered by simulation):

1) If a simulation accidentally impacts prod (unlikely but possible): immediately run simctl teardown --force and notify SRE incident channel.
2) Follow the Production Impact Emergency Checklist (linked RB-900).

Implementation roadmap (next 12 months):

- Q1 2025: ship simctl v0.1, create three canonical scenario manifests, build the simulation overview dashboard.
- Q2 2025: integrate sim-run tags into trace sampler, hook run-archive retention policy.
- Q3 2025: operator training pilot across two oncall rotations. Measure detection fidelity.
- Q4 2025: automate basic mitigations and gate auto-safeguard playbook behind runbook approval.

Metrics to track for program health:

- simulation_runs_per_month
- detection_time_p50_p95
- percent_runs_with_complete_artifacts
- operator_pass_rate_after-training

Appendices:

A) Minimal sim manifest example:

---
name: kv-prefix-saturation-example
duration: 00:15:00
traffic_profile: prefix-heavy-replay-v2
resource_stress: [gpu-stress:1]
observability_tag: sim-run/kv-example
operator_checklist: [RB-011]
---

B) Common CLI commands:

- simctl apply -f <manifest.yaml>    # deploys and schedules run
- simctl inject --run <run-id> --phase=1   # advance to injection
- simctl status --run <run-id>   # show run state and phases
- simctl teardown --run <run-id> --force   # immediate cleanup

C) Contacts and escalation paths:

- Platform Operations Pager: #platform-ops (Slack)
- Serving Runtime lead: Diego Morales (diego@redwood.ai)
- SRE oncall: see rota page /confluence/eng-sre/oncall-rotations

Revision history:

- 2025-02-14: Draft created by Ava Chen.
- 2025-05-08: Added GPU interference scenario and sample alert rule. (Reviewer: Priya Nair)
- 2025-11-09: Polished operator curriculum and retention policy. (Reviewer: Liam O'Sullivan)

source.title: Fidelity budgeting and runtime cost governor manual
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Overview
========
This document defines the Fidelity Budgeting model and the Runtime Cost Governor (RCG) — a declarative control plane that enforces per-tenant/route fidelity budgets at serving time. The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend across mixed workloads.

Intended audience
- Runtime and scheduler engineers
- Product owners for Dedicated/Private customers
- SRE and capacity-planning teams
- Benchmark and profiling engineers

Why this exists
- Inference cost is variable by model variant, sequence length, batching shape and kernel choices.
- We need a single, auditable mechanism to trade fidelity for cost under pressure (autoscale, thermal events, capacity constraints).
- RCG ties scheduling signals, kernel hints, and quantization/variant routing decisions to a budget and provides a deterministic downgrade strategy.

Principles
- Predictability: budget enforcement must be explainable and reproducible.
- Observability: every fidelity decision must be logged with provenance (trigger, score, pre/post metrics).
- Minimize user impact: preservation of critical tokens and short requests is prioritized over long/low-priority generation.
- Fast rollback: policy changes must be reversible within a single canary window.

Key concepts and terminology
- Fidelity budget: numeric allowance per time window (tokens or token-equivalents) that a tenant/route can spend.
- Fidelity credit: per-request currency derived from model variant cost and sequence projection.
- Degradation ladder: ordered list of fidelity actions (e.g., small-quantize -> shorter-prefix-cache -> model-variant -> response-truncation).
- Governor evaluation window: default 30s sliding window for budget accounting.

Metrics, signals and SLOs
- Primary SLOs: 99th-pct tail latency <= 250ms (interactive tier), ERR_RATE <= 0.5%
- Budget signals: token_consumed, credit_burn_rate, on_gpu_slots, queue_depth, kernel_wait_time_ms
- Observability artifacts: fidelity_decision events (json), pre/post latency histograms, regression score (quality delta vs baseline prompts).

Architecture overview
- Inputs: request classifier (priority, route), model catalog pricing table (tokens / cost), runtime telemetry (slot usage, kernel queue), profiling hints (sequence length distribution).
- Decision engine: lightweight policy evaluator that produces an ordered list of degradation actions and an urgency score.
- Enforcers: scheduler (admission control and slot assignment), kernel adapter (per-request kernel flags), router (model variant routing), kvcache layer (prefix eviction hints).
- Audit sink: fidelity-decision store and metrics exporter.

Budgeting controls and policy primitives
- static_budget: fixed tokens per-minute (example: 1e6 tokens/min)
- proportional_budget: fraction of cluster budget for shared pools (0.02 = 2%)
- emergency_buffer: reserved credits only used when latency or error budgets are breached

Simple policy example (YAML)
--------------------------
policy_name: interactive-default
owner: team-runtime
window_seconds: 30
budget_type: token_quota
budget_amount: 50000
degradation_ladder:
  - action: prefer-quantized-variant
    min_quality_delta: -0.02
  - action: reduce-max-output-tokens
    reduction_percent: 25
  - action: route-to-small-variant
    acceptable_loss: -0.05
priority_overrides:
  - route: /chat/completion
    min_reserve: 2000

Scheduler integration
- Admission checks use the RCG decision as a prefilter. If a tenant is above budget, admission returns PROBATION with a recommended action list.
- Scheduler applies soft-slot labels (e.g., fidelity=low) to place downgraded requests on consolidation pools to reduce interference.
- Kernel hints are conveyed via request metadata: {kernel_hint: "prefetch_off,quantize_a2"}.

Profiling & benchmarking guidance
- For each candidate policy change run:
  1) Local microbenchmark with synthetic sequences (short, medium, long) to measure latency and kernel divergence.
  2) Shadow traffic pass (1 hour minimum) to observe real distribution effects.
  3) Quality regression suite (prompt set) measuring ROUGE/BLEU and model-specific evals.
- Required artifacts to land a policy: before/after cost-per-token, latency p50/p95/p99, quality regressions per prompt set, and fidelity_decision trace samples.

Operational runbook: budget breach (quick triage)
1) Detection
  - Alert: fidelity_budget_burn_rate > 120% for 1 minute or fidelity_decision_failure_count > 5/min
2) Immediate actions (first 5 minutes)
  - Escalate to runtime-oncall and set channel #runtime-fidelity with incident tag.
  - Switch policy to safe-mode: static_budget=nominal/4, enforce-first-ladder-only.
  - Create shadow traces for 5 minutes: set header X-Trace-Fidelity-Detail=true.
3) Investigation (5–30 minutes)
  - Check recent deployments: kernel or scheduler changes in the last 30 minutes (kubectl/CI links).
  - Correlate with capacity metrics: GPU_utilization, kernel_wait_time_ms, queue_depth.
  - Run profiling job: sample 2% of traffic for deep trace (use trace-capture CLI below).
4) Mitigation (30–120 minutes)
  - If degraded by thermal or hardware: move high-priority tenants to reserved pools.
  - If caused by regression: revert policy or kernel flag and roll forward safe-mode config.
  - If intentional load spike: negotiate temporary budget increase with product (document in audit).
5) Postmortem requirements
  - Within 48 hours: publish summary, root cause, timeline, and any policy code changes.

Trace capture CLI (example)
- - -
# Capture 10k fidelity_decision events for tenant=acme
runtctl trace capture --tenant acme --events fidelity_decision --limit 10000 --out /tmp/acme-fidelity-trace.ndjson

Policy rollout checklist
1) Author policy with rationale and expected cost delta.
2) Unit tests for policy parser and fallback actions.
3) Local benchmark (p50/p99, cost delta).
4) Canary deploy (1% traffic, 30m), monitor fidelity_decisions and latency SLOs.
5) 10% shadow for 2 hours, examine quality regressions.
6) Gradual 20% -> 50% -> 100% steps with automated rollback thresholds (p99 latency +10% or error_rate > 0.2%).

Example governance matrix (text table)
- Name: interactive-default | Owner: team-runtime | Window: 30s | Budget: 50k tokens
- Rollout gate: p99_latency_delta <= +10ms & quality_regression <= 1%

Telemetry and audit
- fidelity_decision schema (logged): request_id, tenant_id, route, budget_window_start, budget_remaining, chosen_action, reason_code, decision_score, timestamp
- Export to: metrics (Prom), events (kafka topic: fidelity-decisions), long-term store for 90d.

Ownership & contacts
- Policy author: Ava Chen (runtime@redwood.ai)
- Scheduler integration owner: Marcus Patel
- Oncall for fidelity incidents: runtime-oncall rotation (oncall@redwood.ai)

Appendix A — configuration samples
- Governor config (JSON snippet):
{
  "policy_name":"interactive-default",
  "window_seconds":30,
  "budget_amount":50000,
  "degradation_ladder":["prefer-quantized-variant","reduce-max-output-tokens","route-to-small-variant"]
}

Appendix B — quality regression example
- For the "prefer-quantized-variant" action we allow a maximum QA score delta of -0.03 as measured on the interactive-quality prompt set. If measured delta exceeds threshold during canary, the canary aborts.

Related work and references
- See runtime-architecture predictive-prefetch doc for sequence projection inputs.
- See benchmarking suite docs for how to generate the required before/after artifacts.

Change log
- 2025-01-15: created (A. Chen)
- 2025-06-12: added emergency_buffer section and scheduler hints (M. Patel)
- 2025-10-02: clarified rollout gates and updated trace capture CLI (L. Ortiz)

End of document

    min_quality_delta: -0.02
  - action: reduce-max-output-tokens
    reduction_percent: 25
  - action: route-to-small-variant
    acceptable_loss: -0.05
priority_overrides:
  - route: /chat/completion
    min_reserve: 2000

Scheduler integration
- Admission checks use the RCG decision as a prefilter. If a tenant is above budget, admission returns PROBATION with a recommended action list.
- Scheduler applies soft-slot labels (e.g., fidelity=low) to place downgraded requests on consolidation pools to reduce interference.
- Kernel hints are conveyed via request metadata: {kernel_hint: "prefetch_off,quantize_a2"}.

Profiling & benchmarking guidance
- For each candidate policy change run:
  1) Local microbenchmark with synthetic sequences (short, medium, long) to measure latency and kernel divergence.
  2) Shadow traffic pass (1 hour minimum) to observe real distribution effects.
  3) Quality regression suite (prompt set) measuring ROUGE/BLEU and model-specific evals.
- Required artifacts to land a policy: before/after cost-per-token, latency p50/p95/p99, quality regressions per prompt set, and fidelity_decision trace samples.

Operational runbook: budget breach (quick triage)
1) Detection
  - Alert: fidelity_budget_burn_rate > 120% for 1 minute or fidelity_decision_failure_count > 5/min
2) Immediate actions (first 5 minutes)
  - Escalate to runtime-oncall and set channel #runtime-fidelity with incident tag.
  - Switch policy to safe-mode: static_budget=nominal/4, enforce-first-ladder-only.
  - Create shadow traces for 5 minutes: set header X-Trace-Fidelity-Detail=true.
3) Investigation (5–30 minutes)
  - Check recent deployments: kernel or scheduler changes in the last 30 minutes (kubectl/CI links).
  - Correlate with capacity metrics: GPU_utilization, kernel_wait_time_ms, queue_depth.
  - Run profiling job: sample 2% of traffic for deep trace (use trace-capture CLI below).
4) Mitigation (30–120 minutes)
  - If degraded by thermal or hardware: move high-priority tenants to reserved pools.
  - If caused by regression: revert policy or kernel flag and roll forward safe-mode config.
  - If intentional load spike: negotiate temporary budget increase with product (document in audit).
5) Postmortem requirements
  - Within 48 hours: publish summary, root cause, timeline, and any policy code changes.

Trace capture CLI (example)
- - -
# Capture 10k fidelity_decision events for tenant=acme
runtctl trace capture --tenant acme --events fidelity_decision --limit 10000 --out /tmp/acme-fidelity-trace.ndjson

Policy rollout checklist
1) Author policy with rationale and expected cost delta.
2) Unit tests for policy parser and fallback actions.
3) Local benchmark (p50/p99, cost delta).
4) Canary deploy (1% traffic, 30m), monitor fidelity_decisions and latency SLOs.
5) 10% shadow for 2 hours, examine quality regressions.
6) Gradual 20% -> 50% -> 100% steps with automated rollback thresholds (p99 latency +10% or error_rate > 0.2%).

Example governance matrix (text table)
- Name: interactive-default | Owner: team-runtime | Window: 30s | Budget: 50k tokens
- Rollout gate: p99_latency_delta <= +10ms & quality_regression <= 1%

Telemetry and audit
- fidelity_decision schema (logged): request_id, tenant_id, route, budget_window_start, budget_remaining, chosen_action, reason_code, decision_score, timestamp
- Export to: metrics (Prom), events (kafka topic: fidelity-decisions), long-term store for 90d.

Ownership & contacts
- Policy author: Ava Chen (runtime@redwood.ai)
- Scheduler integration owner: Marcus Patel
- Oncall for fidelity incidents: runtime-oncall rotation (oncall@redwood.ai)

Appendix A — configuration samples
- Governor config (JSON snippet):
{
  "policy_name":"interactive-default",
  "window_seconds":30,
  "budget_amount":50000,
  "degradation_ladder":["prefer-quantized-variant","reduce-max-output-tokens","route-to-small-variant"]
}

Appendix B — quality regression example
- For the "prefer-quantized-variant" action we allow a maximum QA score delta of -0.03 as measured on the interactive-quality prompt set. If measured delta exceeds threshold during canary, the canary aborts.

Related work and references
- See runtime-architecture predictive-prefetch doc for sequence projection inputs.
- See benchmarking suite docs for how to generate the required before/after artifacts.

Change log
- 2025-01-15: created (A. Chen)
- 2025-06-12: added emergency_buffer section and scheduler hints (M. Patel)
- 2025-10-02: clarified rollout gates and updated trace capture CLI (L. Ortiz)

End of document
text:
Overview
========
This document defines the Fidelity Budgeting model and the Runtime Cost Governor (RCG) — a declarative control plane that enforces per-tenant/route fidelity budgets at serving time. The goal of the RCG is to keep customer-visible SLOs within targets while bounding per-token and per-request spend across mixed workloads.

Intended audience
- Runtime and scheduler engineers
- Product owners for Dedicated/Private customers
- SRE and capacity-planning teams
- Benchmark and profiling engineers

Why this exists
- Inference cost is variable by model variant, sequence length, batching shape and kernel choices.
- We need a single, auditable mechanism to trade fidelity for cost under pressure (autoscale, thermal events, capacity constraints).
- RCG ties scheduling signals, kernel hints, and quantization/variant routing decisions to a budget and provides a deterministic downgrade strategy.

Principles
- Predictability: budget enforcement must be explainable and reproducible.
- Observability: every fidelity decision must be logged with provenance (trigger, score, pre/post metrics).
- Minimize user impact: preservation of critical tokens and short requests is prioritized over long/low-priority generation.
- Fast rollback: policy changes must be reversible within a single canary window.

Key concepts and terminology
- Fidelity budget: numeric allowance per time window (tokens or token-equivalents) that a tenant/route can spend.
- Fidelity credit: per-request currency derived from model variant cost and sequence projection.
- Degradation ladder: ordered list of fidelity actions (e.g., small-quantize -> shorter-prefix-cache -> model-variant -> response-truncation).
- Governor evaluation window: default 30s sliding window for budget accounting.

Metrics, signals and SLOs
- Primary SLOs: 99th-pct tail latency <= 250ms (interactive tier), ERR_RATE <= 0.5%
- Budget signals: token_consumed, credit_burn_rate, on_gpu_slots, queue_depth, kernel_wait_time_ms
- Observability artifacts: fidelity_decision events (json), pre/post latency histograms, regression score (quality delta vs baseline prompts).

Architecture overview
- Inputs: request classifier (priority, route), model catalog pricing table (tokens / cost), runtime telemetry (slot usage, kernel queue), profiling hints (sequence length distribution).
- Decision engine: lightweight policy evaluator that produces an ordered list of degradation actions and an urgency score.
- Enforcers: scheduler (admission control and slot assignment), kernel adapter (per-request kernel flags), router (model variant routing), kvcache layer (prefix eviction hints).
- Audit sink: fidelity-decision store and metrics exporter.

Budgeting controls and policy primitives
- static_budget: fixed tokens per-minute (example: 1e6 tokens/min)
- proportional_budget: fraction of cluster budget for shared pools (0.02 = 2%)
- emergency_buffer: reserved credits only used when latency or error budgets are breached

Simple policy example (YAML)
--------------------------
policy_name: interactive-default
owner: team-runtime
window_seconds: 30
budget_type: token_quota
budget_amount: 50000
degradation_ladder:
  - action: prefer-quantized-variant
    min_quality_delta: -0.02
  - action: reduce-max-output-tokens
    reduction_percent: 25
  - action: route-to-small-variant
    acceptable_loss: -0.05
priority_overrides:
  - route: /chat/completion
    min_reserve: 2000

Scheduler integration
- Admission checks use the RCG decision as a prefilter. If a tenant is above budget, admission returns PROBATION with a recommended action list.
- Scheduler applies soft-slot labels (e.g., fidelity=low) to place downgraded requests on consolidation pools to reduce interference.
- Kernel hints are conveyed via request metadata: {kernel_hint: "prefetch_off,quantize_a2"}.

Profiling & benchmarking guidance
- For each candidate policy change run:
  1) Local microbenchmark with synthetic sequences (short, medium, long) to measure latency and kernel divergence.
  2) Shadow traffic pass (1 hour minimum) to observe real distribution effects.
  3) Quality regression suite (prompt set) measuring ROUGE/BLEU and model-specific evals.
- Required artifacts to land a policy: before/after cost-per-token, latency p50/p95/p99, quality regressions per prompt set, and fidelity_decision trace samples.

Operational runbook: budget breach (quick triage)
1) Detection
  - Alert: fidelity_budget_burn_rate > 120% for 1 minute or fidelity_decision_failure_count > 5/min
2) Immediate actions (first 5 minutes)
  - Escalate to runtime-oncall and set channel #runtime-fidelity with incident tag.
  - Switch policy to safe-mode: static_budget=nominal/4, enforce-first-ladder-only.
  - Create shadow traces for 5 minutes: set header X-Trace-Fidelity-Detail=true.
3) Investigation (5–30 minutes)
  - Check recent deployments: kernel or scheduler changes in the last 30 minutes (kubectl/CI links).
  - Correlate with capacity metrics: GPU_utilization, kernel_wait_time_ms, queue_depth.
  - Run profiling job: sample 2% of traffic for deep trace (use trace-capture CLI below).
4) Mitigation (30–120 minutes)
  - If degraded by thermal or hardware: move high-priority tenants to reserved pools.
  - If caused by regression: revert policy or kernel flag and roll forward safe-mode config.
  - If intentional load spike: negotiate temporary budget increase with product (document in audit).
5) Postmortem requirements
  - Within 48 hours: publish summary, root cause, timeline, and any policy code changes.

Trace capture CLI (example)
- - -
# Capture 10k fidelity_decision events for tenant=acme
runtctl trace capture --tenant acme --events fidelity_decision --limit 10000 --out /tmp/acme-fidelity-trace.ndjson

Policy rollout checklist
1) Author policy with rationale and expected cost delta.
2) Unit tests for policy parser and fallback actions.
3) Local benchmark (p50/p99, cost delta).
4) Canary deploy (1% traffic, 30m), monitor fidelity_decisions and latency SLOs.
5) 10% shadow for 2 hours, examine quality regressions.
6) Gradual 20% -> 50% -> 100% steps with automated rollback thresholds (p99 latency +10% or error_rate > 0.2%).

Example governance matrix (text table)
- Name: interactive-default | Owner: team-runtime | Window: 30s | Budget: 50k tokens
- Rollout gate: p99_latency_delta <= +10ms & quality_regression <= 1%

Telemetry and audit
- fidelity_decision schema (logged): request_id, tenant_id, route, budget_window_start, budget_remaining, chosen_action, reason_code, decision_score, timestamp
- Export to: metrics (Prom), events (kafka topic: fidelity-decisions), long-term store for 90d.

Ownership & contacts
- Policy author: Ava Chen (runtime@redwood.ai)
- Scheduler integration owner: Marcus Patel
- Oncall for fidelity incidents: runtime-oncall rotation (oncall@redwood.ai)

Appendix A — configuration samples
- Governor config (JSON snippet):
{
  "policy_name":"interactive-default",
  "window_seconds":30,
  "budget_amount":50000,
  "degradation_ladder":["prefer-quantized-variant","reduce-max-output-tokens","route-to-small-variant"]
}

Appendix B — quality regression example
- For the "prefer-quantized-variant" action we allow a maximum QA score delta of -0.03 as measured on the interactive-quality prompt set. If measured delta exceeds threshold during canary, the canary aborts.

Related work and references
- See runtime-architecture predictive-prefetch doc for sequence projection inputs.
- See benchmarking suite docs for how to generate the required before/after artifacts.

- 2025-01-15: created (A. Chen)
- 2025-06-12: added emergency_buffer section and scheduler hints (M. Patel)

source.title: third-party-model-dependency-failure-playbook-2025
source.author: Ava Chen
source.summary: Runbook for detecting, triaging, and mitigating failures that originate in third-party model dependencies (hosted models, external model APIs, or partner model endpoints). Focuses on detection signals, automated mitigations (circuit breakers, model swaps), operator procedures, and post-incident evaluation to reduce customer impact and cost exposure.
source.audience: Platform operators, on-call engineers, SREs, runtime product owners, and solutions engineers who manage model routing and fallbacks.
source.scope: Covers failures where Redwood routes inference requests to a non-Redwood-hosted model provider or a partner-hosted model endpoint, including: API errors (5xx/4xx), rate-limits, latency degradation, credential/PKI failures, contract/version mismatch, and output-quality regression attributed to the external model. Does NOT cover internal runtime kernel bugs, GPU host-level issues, or networking blackholes between Redwood infra components (see separate runbooks).
source.prerequisites: Operators must have active access to the platform operator console, routing control API, tenant routing policies, and playbook runbook-run privileges. Knowledge of the model catalog entries (model-id, variant tags), Canary routing, and feature-flag controls is required.
source.alerting_thresholds_table:
| Signal | Metric | Alert threshold (warning) | Alert threshold (critical) | Suggested action |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| Provider error rate | 5xx ratio from provider | >= 1% over 2m | >= 5% over 1m | Trigger circuit breaker; route to fallback |
| Upstream latency | p95 latency to provider | > 500ms sustained 3m | > 1s sustained 1m | Consider traffic split to local/hosted variant |
| Rate limit responses | 429 count | > 10/min | > 50/min | Throttle clients; engage provider |
| Quality regression | Eval failure rate vs baseline | +2% drift over 1h | +5% drift over 30m | Roll traffic to validated model; alert ML team |
| Auth failures | 401/403 to provider | any | repeated | Rotate credentials; fallback to hosted model
detection_signals: Provider-origin 5xx and connection errors surfaced in request logs
Sudden rise in p95/p99 latency for routes flagged as external-models
Increase in 429/rate-limit indicators from provider endpoints
Quality regressions detected by evaluation service (prompt-set failures or hallucination metrics)
Customer-reported errors or SLA burn correlated with routes that map to third-party models
Alert from provider status page or partner Slack channel
immediate_runbook_steps: 1) Triage: Identify affected routes and scope. Use routing dashboard filter: route.type = external-model and time window = last 15m.
2) Confirm signal: Check provider error logs, network connection health, and recent deploys. Correlate with model-catalog changes and feature-flag flips in the last 60 minutes.
3) Short-circuit traffic: If provider error rate is critical, apply circuit-breaker rule for the route to stop forwarding new requests to provider (example REST call below).

4) Route to fallback: Switch traffic to the preconfigured fallback chain in order: (A) Redwood-hosted vetted model variant (smaller, quantized), (B) cached KV prefix responses, (C) synthetic response with user-friendly error and 'try again' header.
5) Notify: Page the model-owner oncall and the partner/third-party integration contact. Create incident in tracker with tags: third-party-model, routing, urgent.
6) Rate-limit and throttle upstream clients to reduce request pressure while we stabilize the provider link (use tenant soft-quota mechanisms).
7) Observe: Monitor quality metrics and customer error rates for 15m after swap. If fallback causes unacceptable quality regression, escalate per escalation_matrix.
dashboards_and_queries: Routing dashboard: Filter by route.tag:external-model, panels: 5xx-rate, p95 latency, 429-count, active-connections
Provider health panel: Synthetic probe p50/p95 to provider endpoint (http status + RTT), located under /dashboards/provider-health
Quality-eval dashboard: Prompt-set pass rate and hallucination score for affected model-id over sliding windows (1h/24h), compare to baseline revision
Kibana query example (errors): service:runtime AND route:\"external-model\" AND response.status:[500 TO 599] AND @timestamp:>=now-15m

SQL example for usage surge: SELECT route_id, count(*) AS qps, sum(case when status>=500 then 1 else 0 end) as errors FROM requests WHERE time > now() - INTERVAL '15 minutes' GROUP BY route_id ORDER BY qps DESC;
escalation_matrix: Level 0 (Operator): Implement circuit-breaker and apply routing swap. Contact model-owner and platform-pager.
Level 1 (Model Owner): If swap not possible or quality unknown, model-owner to validate fallback artifacts and coordinate with partner ops within 30 minutes.
Level 2 (Partner/Provider): Engage third-party support (open P1) and exchange logs, timestamps, and auth traces. Request provider rate-limit increases or emergency key rotation as needed.
Level 3 (Product/Security): If issue is credential compromise or legal-sensitive, page security@ and product legal within 15 minutes of detection.
SLA Notification: For enterprise customers affected beyond 30 minutes, notify Customer Success and trigger AB status/update cadence.
fallback_sequence: Primary fallback (automated): Redwood-hosted vetted model variant (model-id: <hosted-variant>), routed via policy: priority=fallback, preserve request headers and kv-prefix-cache.
Secondary fallback (cached): Serve cached prefix outputs for identical prompt patterns (use TTL=5m), mark response header X-Redwood-Fallback: cached
Tertiary fallback (degraded output): Return structured error message with polite degrade and suggested retry-after; include tokenized stub to reduce client confusion
Manual emergency: If automated fallbacks fail, use manual routing override in operator console: set route.override_to = <internal-smaller-model> and throttle external traffic by 50%
quality_guardrails: Run post-swap quick-evals against 20 representative prompts immediately after reroute. Minimum pass rate: 90% of baseline pass rate.
If quality drift > 5% for critical prompt set, rollback to provider only under explicit product approval and annotate incident with rationale.
Record examples of hallucinations or semantic regressions in evidence bundle for model team triage.
Automate synthetic test suite to run every 5m for routes with external-models when traffic is > 1r/s.
postmortem_checklist: Collect timeline: first alert, operator actions, partner responses, traffic swaps, final resolution time.
Quantify impact: customer minutes of degraded service, SLA burn, token-cost delta from reroutes, and any data exfiltration concerns.
Root cause analysis: provider-side, connectivity, auth, misconfiguration, or model-quality regression.
Action items: credential rotation automation, additional health probes, fallback verification in canary, and SLA change proposals with partner.
Owners for remediation tasks with due dates and verification criteria.
owners_and_contacts: Model owner: team-llm-runtime@redwood.ai (Primary: Ava Chen avachen@redwood.ai)
Partner ops contact: partner-ops@example-partner.com (use escalation channel: +1-555-0102)
runbook_examples_and_commands: 1) Apply circuit breaker via routing API (example):
incident create --title 'Third-party model errors: <route-id>' --severity P1 --labels third-party,model --body 'Summary, timeline, immediate actions taken, evidence bundle: s3://incidents/<id>'
training_and_readiness: Runbook drill cadence: Quarterly table-top exercises including model-swap drills and partner contact verification.
Controlled chaos: Simulate provider 429/errors in staging for two routes every release cycle to validate automatic fallback behavior.
Operator certification: Operators must demonstrate a successful guided swap and post-swap quality check to be certified.
references: Model catalog: internal link to model-id metadata (see model-catalog page)
source.body:
Overview:
This playbook documents the operator workflow when an external model provider (third-party model API or partner-hosted endpoint) causes production failures. It is written for platform operators who can apply routing changes and coordinate with model owners and partners.

Sections included:
- Detection signals and dashboards
- Immediate operator runbook steps (triage, circuit-breaker, fallback, notify)
- Escalation matrix and contacts
- Quality guardrails and quick-eval requirements
- Postmortem and remediation checklist

Guiding principles:
1) Prefer automated, well-tested fallbacks to manual remediation when possible.
2) Preserve customer-facing semantics (errors, retry headers) so clients have predictable behavior.
3) Protect cost budgets during partner outage by throttling non-critical tenants.
4) Capture evidence for the model team so regressions can be diagnosed (request ids, example prompts).

Operator quick checklist (1-line):
- Confirm provider 5xx/latency -> open circuit -> switch to hosted fallback -> page model-owner and partner -> monitor quality metrics -> escalate if necessary.

Table of recommended SLO changes for external-model routes (operational suggestion):
- Maintain synthetic probe p95 < 600ms for production; if probe > 1s for 3 probes, preemptively route 50% traffic to fallback.

Evidence bundle requirements to attach to incident ticket:
- 1) Sample of 20 failed requests (request-id, timestamp, route-id, upstream response code and body)
- 2) Routing policy snapshot before and after swap
- 3) Quality-eval diffs from pre/post swap
- 4) Provider status page logs or support ticket references

Post-incident actions (example):
- Automate additional health probes for the provider if degradation was transient but repeated.
- Add automated smoke tests for fallback models to continuous canary pipeline.
- Negotiate provider SLA/credits if repeated outages exceed contractual thresholds.

Revision history:
- 2025-03-10 Created by Ava Chen
- 2025-09-12 Added credential-rotation escalation and updated curl examples
