source.title: Responder Onboarding, Handoff, and Game-Day Runway
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Overview

This document describes a compact, repeatable process for responder onboarding, shift handoffs, and the operational runway used during game-day exercises and real incidents. It is targeted at first/second-line responders and incident commanders for Redwood Inference services, with actionable checklists, communication templates, and a short set of measurable acceptance criteria for readiness checks.

Scope

- Applies to Redwood production and staging environments that support the Redwood API, Dedicated, and Private control planes.
- Focuses on human workflow: oncall orientation, handoff, escalation cadence, game-day runway, and customer-facing communication templates.
- Does NOT replace service-specific runbooks; links to them are included in "Related runbooks" below.

Key goals

1. Reduce cognitive load during shift starts and incident escalation.
2. Ensure consistent, low-friction customer notifications.
3. Provide a repeatable game-day scenario and rollback runway for training.

Roles & Responsibilities

- First Responder (FR): Owns initial triage, evidence collection, short-term mitigation (10–30m).
- Service Owner (SO): Responsible for service-level decisions and follow-up.
- Incident Commander (IC): Runs the bridge, coordinates cross-functional work, owns triage to restoration and comm cadence.
- Escalation SME: Deep subject matter expert (runtime, networking, or infra) brought in for targeted fixes.
- Communications Lead: Drafts and posts status-page updates and external notices.

Activation & Triage Flow (high-level)

1) Alert received and acknowledged (0–5m)
   - FR acknowledges in pager/PagerDuty and posts initial message to #incident-bridge with service tag.
   - If alert correlates with an ongoing incident (same incident ID), add to existing bridge; otherwise start new bridge.

2) Quick evidence & impact assessment (5–15m)
   - Collect: top-5 relevant logs, top host/container metrics, recent deploys, and active traffic spike indicators.
   - Determine impact tier (see Incident Classification Matrix).

3) Escalate to IC if impact tier >= P2 or if restoration is not possible within 15 minutes.

4) IC runs restoration play; Communications Lead posts initial customer notice within 15–30 minutes for P1/P2 incidents.

Incident Classification Matrix

| Tier | Description | Typical SLO | Initial comm window |
|------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| P1   | Total outage for customers or severe latency (>3x SLO) affecting >=50% of traffic | Availability 99.9% | 15 minutes |
| P2   | Significant degradation impacting a subset of customers or latency beyond SLO for critical endpoints | Availability 99.95% | 30 minutes |
| P3   | Partial feature impact, degraded non-critical endpoints | N/A | Next business hour |
| P4   | Informational / scheduled maintenance | N/A | N/A |

On-call Handoff Checklist (to be completed during shift transition)

- Pre-handoff (outgoing):
  - Update the personal handoff doc with any ongoing alerts, mitigations, and active tickets.
  - Confirm there are no pending deploys or maintenance windows that overlap the next shift.
  - Ensure bridge links and runbook links are up-to-date in the ticket.

- Handoff meeting (5–10 minutes):
  1. Outgoing: 60-second summary of current state and top risks.
  2. Incoming: Read back the summary and confirm understanding.
  3. Optional: Run a 2-minute readiness check (see Readiness checklist below).

- Post-handoff (incoming):
  - Mark the rotation change in PagerDuty and Slack status.
  - Create or update any follow-up tickets for tasks with >1h work.

Readiness checklist (2-minute smoke test for incoming responder)

- Confirm you can:
  - Authenticate to the internal console and access the incident bridge.
  - Query the main metrics dashboard (latency, error rate, token/sec).
  - Read/write to the status page publisher tool.
  - Trigger a no-op healthcheck call to the service (curl example below).

Example healthcheck command:

```
curl -sSf -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" https://api.redwood.example/v0/health || echo 'healthcheck failed'
```

Evidence Collection Template (first 10 minutes)

- Timestamps of first alert(s) and earliest anomalous metric.
- Top 3 log snippets correlated to the spike (service, pod, region).
- Recent deploy SHA and deploy owner.
- Last 10 minutes of metrics snapshot (latency p50/p95/p99, error rates, queue lengths).
- Any customer tickets referencing the same timeframe.

Communication Templates

1) Internal Bridge Opener (Slack)

```
[IC] Bridge open for: api-serving-prod - high latency incident.
Symptoms: increased p95 and 5xx spikes in us-west.
Impact: ~40% of requests elevated latency / 8% 5xx.
Initial owner: @first-responder. Bridge: <link>. Status page ticket: #12345.
```

2) Initial Customer Notice (public status page) - short template

Summary: We are investigating elevated latency affecting text-generation endpoints in us-west.
Impact: Some requests may experience increased latency or timeouts.
What we are doing: Engineers are actively triaging and applying mitigations.
Next update: within 30 minutes or when we have a material change.

3) Status Update (public) - follow-up template

Summary: Mitigation applied (traffic throttling / fallback to variant model X).
Impact: Error rate reduced from 8% to 1.2% for affected customers; latency improved but p95 remains elevated.
What we are doing: We're validating quality and rolling incremental fixes; next update in 60 minutes.

4) Customer Post-Incident Notice (postmortem summary)

Summary of event, impact window, affected models/regions, root cause (short), customer-facing mitigations applied, and links to more detailed postmortem.

Game-Day Runway (exercise template)

Objective: Validate handoff discipline, comm cadence, and rollback/runforward playbook under a controlled degradation.

Scenario examples (pick one per exercise):
- Scenario A: KV cache eviction causes p95 latency to jump in a single region.
- Scenario B: Batching subsystem stalls due to a kernel regression at t=0.
- Scenario C: Upstream datastore throttles queries for 20 minutes.

Runbook for a 90-minute game-day exercise

1. 0–5m: Inject synthetic alert (monitoring team). Responders acknowledge and open bridge.
2. 5–15m: FR collects evidence and classifies incident. Communications Lead posts initial status.
3. 15–30m: IC decides mitigation: rollback last deploy or apply traffic reroute.
4. 30–60m: Implement mitigation and measure recovery.
5. 60–75m: Execute a controlled rollback or model fallback; confirm customer-impact metrics.
6. 75–90m: Post-incident briefing and draft a compact postmortem; collect action items and assign owners.

Runway checklist (pre-game-day)

- Confirm participants and roles; set expectations.
- Reserve an incident bridge and status page slot.
- Ensure synthetic traffic generator is configured and able to emulate 10–30% load.
- Prepare fail-safe rollback steps and a quick stop command.

Rollback and Hotfix Orchestration (short SOP)

- Prefer automated, small-step rollbacks with health gates.
- If rollback is manual:
  1. Confirm rollback SHA and author.
  2. Notify IC and Communications Lead.
  3. Execute rollback to the last verified-good SHA and validate health checks for 5 minutes.
  4. If health checks fail, immediately revert to pre-rollback state and contact Core Infra SME.

Escalation Ladder

- 0–15 minutes: First Responder handles.
- 15–45 minutes: Escalate to Service Owner and IC.
- 45+ minutes or high customer impact: Involve Eng Director and Legal/PR for potential external comms.

Postmortem & Action Tracking (compact)

- Draft postmortem within 48 hours. Include: timeline, impact, root cause hypothesis, mitigations, and three clear action items with owners and due dates.
- Use standard postmortem template: Summary, Chronology, Root Cause, Mitigations, Action Items, Learnings.
- Assign a follow-up review 2 weeks after incident to verify action item completion.

Metrics & Readiness KPIs

- Average handoff duration: target <= 10 minutes.
- First-contact acknowledgment time: target <= 2 minutes.
- Initial customer notice time for P1/P2: target <= 30 minutes.
- Game-day run frequency: at least once per quarter for each service team.

Operational tooling & quick references

- Incident bridge: https://zoom.redwood.example/incident-bridge
- Status page publisher CLI: /usr/local/bin/status-publisher
- Healthcheck endpoint pattern: https://api.redwood.example/v0/{service}/health
- PagerDuty play: "Redwood: Incident responder"

Quick CLI examples

Post a status update via CLI (internal):
```
status-publisher post --component api-serving --summary "Investigating elevated latency in us-west" --visibility public
```

Open a minimal evidence bundle (for ticket):
```
redwood-ops collect-evidence --service api-serving --window 10m --output /tmp/evidence-$(date +%s).tgz
```

Appendix: Common pitfalls & dos/don'ts

- DO keep initial public notices brief and factual.
- DO assign owners for mitigation actions immediately.
- DO practice the runway at least quarterly.
- DON'T speculate about root cause in customer-facing comms.
- DON'T delay initiating mitigations while waiting for verbose evidence if impact is high.

Related runbooks & pages

- Service-specific runtime runbooks (links listed in the team wiki).
- Incident comm and postmortem framework (see related pages above).
- Oncall simulation cookbook for KV/cache scenarios.

Ownership & escalation contacts

- Document owner: Ava Chen (Engineering) — ava@redwood.example
- Pager duty team: Redwood-core-oncall@redwood.example
- Communications Lead: Nina Patel — nina@redwood.example

Change log

- 2024-11-15: Created initial draft.
- 2025-03-02: Added Game-Day Runway and rollback SOP.
- 2025-06-10: Clarified readiness checklist and communication templates.

End of document

title: Responder Onboarding, Handoff, and Game-Day Runway
preview:
Overview

This document describes a compact, repeatable process for responder onboarding, shift handoffs, and the operational runway used during game-day exercises and real incidents. It is targeted at first/second-line responders and incident commanders for Redwood Inference services, with actionable checklists, communication templates, and a short set of measurable acceptance criteria for readiness checks.

Scope

- Applies to Redwood production and staging environments that support the Redwood API, Dedicated, and Private control planes.
- Focuses on human workflow: oncall orientation, handoff, escalation cadence, game-day runway, and customer-facing communication templates.
- Does NOT replace service-specific runbooks; links to them are included in "Related runbooks" below.

Key goals

1. Reduce cognitive load during shift starts and incident escalation.
2. Ensure consistent, low-friction customer notifications.
3. Provide a repeatable game-day scenario and rollback runway for training.

Roles & Responsibilities

- First Responder (FR): Owns initial triage, evidence collection, short-term mitigation (10–30m).
- Service Owner (SO): Responsible for service-level decisions and follow-up.
- Incident Commander (IC): Runs the bridge, coordinates cross-functional work, owns triage to restoration and comm cadence.
- Escalation SME: Deep subject matter expert (runtime, networking, or infra) brought in for targeted fixes.
- Communications Lead: Drafts and posts status-page updates and external notices.

Activation & Triage Flow (high-level)

1) Alert received and acknowledged (0–5m)
   - FR acknowledges in pager/PagerDuty and posts initial message to #incident-bridge with service tag.
   - If alert correlates with an ongoing incident (same incident ID), add to existing bridge; otherwise start new bridge.

2) Quick evidence & impact assessment (5–15m)
   - Collect: top-5 relevant logs, top host/container metrics, recent deploys, and active traffic spike indicators.
   - Determine impact tier (see Incident Classification Matrix).

3) Escalate to IC if impact tier >= P2 or if restoration is not possible within 15 minutes.

4) IC runs restoration play; Communications Lead posts initial customer notice within 15–30 minutes for P1/P2 incidents.

Incident Classification Matrix

| Tier | Description | Typical SLO | Initial comm window |
|------|-------------|-------------|---------------------|
| P1   | Total outage for customers or severe latency (>3x SLO) affecting >=50% of traffic | Availability 99.9% | 15 minutes |
| P2   | Significant degradation impacting a subset of customers or latency beyond SLO for critical endpoints | Availability 99.95% | 30 minutes |
| P3   | Partial feature impact, degraded non-critical endpoints | N/A | Next business hour |
| P4   | Informational / scheduled maintenance | N/A | N/A |

On-call Handoff Checklist (to be completed during shift transition)

- Pre-handoff (outgoing):
  - Update the personal handoff doc with any ongoing alerts, mitigations, and active tickets.
  - Confirm there are no pending deploys or maintenance windows that overlap the next shift.
  - Ensure bridge links and runbook links are up-to-date in the ticket.

- Handoff meeting (5–10 minutes):
  1. Outgoing: 60-second summary of current state and top risks.
  2. Incoming: Read back the summary and confirm understanding.
  3. Optional: Run a 2-minute readiness check (see Readiness checklist below).

- Post-handoff (incoming):
  - Mark the rotation change in PagerDuty and Slack status.
  - Create or update any follow-up tickets for tasks with >1h work.

Readiness checklist (2-minute smoke test for incoming responder)

- Confirm you can:
  - Authenticate to the internal console and access the incident bridge.
  - Query the main metrics dashboard (latency, error rate, token/sec).
  - Read/write to the status page publisher tool.
  - Trigger a no-op healthcheck call to the service (curl example below).

Example healthcheck command:

```
curl -sSf -H "Authorization: Bearer $REDWOOD_API_KEY" https://api.redwood.example/v0/health || echo 'healthcheck failed'
```

Evidence Collection Template (first 10 minutes)

- Timestamps of first alert(s) and earliest anomalous metric.
- Top 3 log snippets correlated to the spike (service, pod, region).
- Recent deploy SHA and deploy owner.
- Last 10 minutes of metrics snapshot (latency p50/p95/p99, error rates, queue lengths).
- Any customer tickets referencing the same timeframe.

Communication Templates

1) Internal Bridge Opener (Slack)

```
[IC] Bridge open for: api-serving-prod - high latency incident.
Symptoms: increased p95 and 5xx spikes in us-west.
Impact: ~40% of requests elevated latency / 8% 5xx.
Initial owner: @first-responder. Bridge: <link>. Status page ticket: #12345.
```

2) Initial Customer Notice (public status page) - short template

Summary: We are investigating elevated latency affecting text-generation endpoints in us-west.
Impact: Some requests may experience increased latency or timeouts.
What we are doing: Engineers are actively triaging and applying mitigations.
Next update: within 30 minutes or when we have a material change.

3) Status Update (public) - follow-up template

Summary: Mitigation applied (traffic throttling / fallback to variant model X).
Impact: Error rate reduced from 8% to 1.2% for affected customers; latency improved but p95 remains elevated.
What we are doing: We're validating quality and rolling incremental fixes; next update in 60 minutes.

4) Customer Post-Incident Notice (postmortem summary)

Summary of event, impact window, affected models/regions, root cause (short), customer-facing mitigations applied, and links to more detailed postmortem.

Game-Day Runway (exercise template)

Objective: Validate handoff discipline, comm cadence, and rollback/runforward playbook under a controlled degradation.

Scenario examples (pick one per exercise):
- Scenario A: KV cache eviction causes p95 latency to jump in a single region.
- Scenario B: Batching subsystem stalls due to a kernel regression at t=0.
- Scenario C: Upstream datastore throttles queries for 20 minutes.

Runbook for a 90-minute game-day exercise

1. 0–5m: Inject synthetic alert (monitoring team). Responders acknowledge and open bridge.
2. 5–15m: FR collects evidence and classifies incident. Communications Lead posts initial status.
3. 15–30m: IC decides mitigation: rollback last deploy or apply traffic reroute.
4. 30–60m: Implement mitigation and measure recovery.
5. 60–75m: Execute a controlled rollback or model fallback; confirm customer-impact metrics.
6. 75–90m: Post-incident briefing and draft a compact postmortem; collect action items and assign owners.

Runway checklist (pre-game-day)

- Confirm participants and roles; set expectations.
- Reserve an incident bridge and status page slot.
- Ensure synthetic traffic generator is configured and able to emulate 10–30% load.
- Prepare fail-safe rollback steps and a quick stop command.

Rollback and Hotfix Orchestration (short SOP)

- Prefer automated, small-step rollbacks with health gates.
- If rollback is manual:
  1. Confirm rollback SHA and author.
  2. Notify IC and Communications Lead.
  3. Execute rollback to the last verified-good SHA and validate health checks for 5 minutes.
  4. If health checks fail, immediately revert to pre-rollback state and contact Core Infra SME.

Escalation Ladder

- 0–15 minutes: First Responder handles.
- 15–45 minutes: Escalate to Service Owner and IC.
- 45+ minutes or high customer impact: Involve Eng Director and Legal/PR for potential external comms.

Postmortem & Action Tracking (compact)

- Draft postmortem within 48 hours. Include: timeline, impact, root cause hypothesis, mitigations, and three clear action items with owners and due dates.
- Use standard postmortem template: Summary, Chronology, Root Cause, Mitigations, Action Items, Learnings.
- Assign a follow-up review 2 weeks after incident to verify action item completion.

Metrics & Readiness KPIs

- Average handoff duration: target <= 10 minutes.
- First-contact acknowledgment time: target <= 2 minutes.
- Initial customer notice time for P1/P2: target <= 30 minutes.
- Game-day run frequency: at least once per quarter for each service team.

Operational tooling & quick references

- Incident bridge: https://zoom.redwood.example/incident-bridge
- Status page publisher CLI: /usr/local/bin/status-publisher
- Healthcheck endpoint pattern: https://api.redwood.example/v0/{service}/health
- PagerDuty play: "Redwood: Incident responder"

Quick CLI examples

Post a status update via CLI (internal):
```
status-publisher post --component api-serving --summary "Investigating elevated latency in us-west" --visibility public
```

Open a minimal evidence bundle (for ticket):
```
redwood-ops collect-evidence --service api-serving --window 10m --output /tmp/evidence-$(date +%s).tgz
```

Appendix: Common pitfalls & dos/don'ts

- DO keep initial public notices brief and factual.
- DO assign owners for mitigation actions immediately.
- DO practice the runway at least quarterly.
- DON'T speculate about root cause in customer-facing comms.
- DON'T delay initiating mitigations while waiting for verbose evidence if impact is high.

Related runbooks & pages

- Service-specific runtime runbooks (links listed in the team wiki).
- Incident comm and postmortem framework (see related pages above).
- Oncall simulation cookbook for KV/cache scenarios.

Ownership & escalation contacts

- Document owner: Ava Chen (Engineering) — ava@redwood.example
- Pager duty team: Redwood-core-oncall@redwood.example
- Communications Lead: Nina Patel — nina@redwood.example

Change log

- 2024-11-15: Created initial draft.
- 2025-03-02: Added Game-Day Runway and rollback SOP.
- 2025-06-10: Clarified readiness checklist and communication templates.

End of document

source.title: INC-2025-0522 Postmortem: Private deployment capacity drop from GPU fragmentation
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
# Summary
On 2025-05-22, a Private (VPC) deployment experienced a 35–45% effective throughput drop and periodic timeouts. The fleet had enough raw GPUs, but fragmentation and placement constraints reduced usable capacity for the target model group.

**Incident ID:** INC-2025-0522
**Customer impact:** Single private customer (enterprise), production
**Owning engineering team:** Eng Infra

# What happened
A model group was pinned to a specific GPU SKU and memory profile. After an unrelated maintenance rotation, the pool contained a mix of GPU SKUs and partially utilized nodes. The scheduler could not place new replicas that met the constraints, leading to queue growth and timeouts.

# Root cause
**Primary:** Fragmentation of allocatable GPU resources under strict placement constraints.
**Contributing factors:**
- Lack of a fragmentation metric and alert
- Runbook did not include defragmentation/evacuation procedure

# Mitigation
- Evacuated partially utilized nodes and rebalanced replicas
- Temporarily loosened placement constraints for non-latency-critical routes

# Corrective actions
1. Add fragmentation metric: "sched_place_fail_rate" + "gpu_mem_slack" (owner: Eng Infra)
2. Publish runbook for defragmentation and safe evacuation in private clusters (owner: Eng Infra)
3. Add preflight capacity validation before maintenance rotations (owner: Eng SRE)

# References
- Jira: INT-601 (internal incident ticket)

source.title: Third‑Party Adapter Compatibility and Safety Standard
source.author: Ava Chen
source.body:
Summary:

This standard prescribes the contract, runtime constraints, and operational hygiene for third‑party adapters (also called plugins or vendor shims) that integrate with Redwood services. The goal is to allow safe, auditable extension of runtime behavior while preserving SLOs, tenant isolation, and traceability.

Why this matters:
- We are exposing stable extension points (for routing, preprocessing, and postprocessing) to third parties and internal partner teams. Without clear compatibility and safety rules these adapters can cause availability incidents, data leaks, and unbounded cost.
- Adapters must be pluggable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments with predictable behavior.

Scope:
- Applies to: adapter binaries, sidecars, WASM modules, and curated vendor integrations that run in the inference path or control plane hooks.
- Excludes: offline ETL plugins, developer CLI plugins, and build‑time artifact converters (governed by separate CI/IaC policies).

Owner and review cadence:
- Standard owner: Runtime Extensions team (eng‑platform).
- Security reviewer: Platform Security.
- Review cadence: annual or when a breaking runtime change is introduced.

Compatibility contract (API surface):
1) Manifest file (required): each adapter MUST include a JSON manifest with the following keys:
   - name (string), version (semver), capabilities (list of strings: preprocess, postprocess, route_hint), hash (sha256 of artifact), min_runtime_version, max_runtime_version, public (bool).

   Example manifest:

   {
     "name": "redwood-spellcheck-adapter",
     "version": "1.2.0",
     "capabilities": ["preprocess", "postprocess"],
     "hash": "6f1a...c9d2",
     "min_runtime_version": "2025.01",
     "max_runtime_version": "2026.12",
     "public": false
   }

2) Endpoint schema: adapters that receive or return JSON payloads MUST document the request/response schema using the contract envelope defined in api-standards/canonical-request-schema-and-validation-playbook-2025.json. Adapters SHOULD avoid adding binary blobs to inference path.

3) Capability negotiation: on registration the runtime validates the manifest and negotiates allowed capabilities. New capabilities require an approver from the Runtime Extensions team and a documented backward‑compatibility strategy.

Safety and resource controls (runtime):
- Execution model: adapters MUST run in one of the supported sandboxes: WASM, sidecar container, or trusted kernel module. The default allowed sandbox is WASM with deterministic resource limits.
- CPU: each adapter execution has a hard CPU quota (default 200ms CPU per request) and soft CPU budget tracked by the adapter token. Exceeding soft budget increments an adapter error counter (see Observability).
- Memory: max resident set limit 64MB for preprocessors, 128MB for postprocessors, configurable per‑customer in private deployments.
- Network: network egress is denied by default. Any egress must be explicitly allowed via an allowance list and reviewed by security.
- File system: readonly mount only; write operations redirected to ephemeral tmpfs with 5s lifetime.
- Timeouts: wallclock timeout 500ms for preprocess, 1000ms for postprocess unless a higher SLA is approved.

Safety and security controls (policy):
- Data handling: adapters must declare PII handling in manifest; access to customer prompt text must be approved and audited. No secret material (keys/tokens) may be embedded in adapter artifacts.
- Signing: vendor artifacts must be signed with an org key; runtime verifies signature and manifest hash.
- RBAC: registration requires a service account with role runtime.adapter.register. Role separation enforced by policy.

Observability and SLOs:
- Mandatory telemetry: adapters must emit structured telemetry for: invocation, error (with taxonomy), duration_ms, memory_mb, and capability_version. Telemetry follows our typed-metric schema.
- Tracing: adapters must propagate the request trace id and add a span with name adapter.<name>.execute. Span duration must capture wallclock time and recorded CPU delta.
- SLO: adapters are included in the parent request 99th percentile latency SLO. Any adapter that causes SLO breach after a canary period will be automatically disabled by the runtime fallback controller.

Testing and certification:
- Unit tests: adapters must include a test manifest and at least five deterministic unit tests covering happy path, timeout, malformed input, and capability downgrade.
- Integration tests: the adapter package must include an integration test invoking the sandboxed runtime in CI and asserting telemetry and span emission.
- Certification matrix: the Runtime Extensions team will publish a compatibility matrix. Certified versions appear in the public model catalog with an extra badge.

Safe rollout and emergency rollback playbook (operational procedure):
1) Staging registration: vendor registers adapter in staging with a limited tenant allowlist (max 3 tenants).
2) Canary traffic: route 0.5–3% of production requests for 48 hours. Monitor: latency_99p, error_rate, memory_peak.
3) Gradual expansion: double fingerprint every 48 hours if no regressions.
4) Emergency rollback: runtime has a kill switch. Operator steps:
   - Acknowledge incident in PagerDuty.
   - Disable adapter via runtime CLI: redwood runtime adapter disable <name> --env=prod
   - If unavailable, circuit the entire extension point using the fallback policy.
5) Postmortem: vendor and Runtime Extensions team conduct joint blameless postmortem within 72 hours.

CLI and example commands:
- Register: redwood runtime adapter register --manifest ./manifest.json --artifact ./adapter.wasm
- Inspect: redwood runtime adapter describe <name> --env=staging
- Disable: redwood runtime adapter disable <name> --reason "slo-regression"

Decision rationale and alternatives considered:
- Why WASM default? WASM provides strong sandboxing and deterministic resource controls. Sidecars are permitted when native dependencies are required, but sidecars require a stricter security review.
- Why deny egress by default? Prevents inadvertent data exfiltration; exceptions are rare and auditable.

Compatibility and upgrade policy:
- Minor manifest version changes with identical semantics are allowed to be hot swapped. Major version bumps must go through the canary rollout above.
- Runtime upgrades that change the adapter ABI require a migration shim or deprecation window of at least 90 days.

Ownership and contacts:
- Runtime Extensions (primary): runtime-extensions@redwood.example.com
- Platform Security (sec review): platform-security@redwood.example.com
- Support runbook: see /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/adapter-fallback-runbook-2025.json

Appendix A — Manifest validation pseudo‑schema:
- name: regex ^[a-z0-9_-]+$
- version: semver 2.0
- capabilities: subset of allowed list [preprocess, postprocess, route_hint, telemetry_enricher]
- hash: sha256
- min_runtime_version, max_runtime_version: semantic tokens

Appendix B — Observability sample metric mapping (CSV):
metric_name,unit,required
adapter.invocations,count,yes
adapter.errors,count,yes
adapter.duration_ms,ms,yes
adapter.memory_mb,mb,yes

title: Third‑Party Adapter Compatibility and Safety Standard
preview:
Summary:

This standard prescribes the contract, runtime constraints, and operational hygiene for third‑party adapters (also called plugins or vendor shims) that integrate with Redwood services. The goal is to allow safe, auditable extension of runtime behavior while preserving SLOs, tenant isolation, and traceability.

Why this matters:
- We are exposing stable extension points (for routing, preprocessing, and postprocessing) to third parties and internal partner teams. Without clear compatibility and safety rules these adapters can cause availability incidents, data leaks, and unbounded cost.
- Adapters must be pluggable across hosted, dedicated, and private deployments with predictable behavior.

Scope:
- Applies to: adapter binaries, sidecars, WASM modules, and curated vendor integrations that run in the inference path or control plane hooks.
- Excludes: offline ETL plugins, developer CLI plugins, and build‑time artifact converters (governed by separate CI/IaC policies).

Owner and review cadence:
- Standard owner: Runtime Extensions team (eng‑platform).
- Security reviewer: Platform Security.
- Review cadence: annual or when a breaking runtime change is introduced.

Compatibility contract (API surface):
1) Manifest file (required): each adapter MUST include a JSON manifest with the following keys:
   - name (string), version (semver), capabilities (list of strings: preprocess, postprocess, route_hint), hash (sha256 of artifact), min_runtime_version, max_runtime_version, public (bool).

   Example manifest:

   {
     \"name\": \"redwood-spellcheck-adapter\",
     \"version\": \"1.2.0\",
     \"capabilities\": [\"preprocess\", \"postprocess\"],
     \"hash\": \"6f1a...c9d2\",
     \"min_runtime_version\": \"2025.01\",
     \"max_runtime_version\": \"2026.12\",
     \"public\": false
   }

2) Endpoint schema: adapters that receive or return JSON payloads MUST document the request/response schema using the contract envelope defined in api-standards/canonical-request-schema-and-validation-playbook-2025.json. Adapters SHOULD avoid adding binary blobs to inference path.

3) Capability negotiation: on registration the runtime validates the manifest and negotiates allowed capabilities. New capabilities require an approver from the Runtime Extensions team and a documented backward‑compatibility strategy.

Safety and resource controls (runtime):
- Execution model: adapters MUST run in one of the supported sandboxes: WASM, sidecar container, or trusted kernel module. The default allowed sandbox is WASM with deterministic resource limits.
- CPU: each adapter execution has a hard CPU quota (default 200ms CPU per request) and soft CPU budget tracked by the adapter token. Exceeding soft budget increments an adapter error counter (see Observability).
- Memory: max resident set limit 64MB for preprocessors, 128MB for postprocessors, configurable per‑customer in private deployments.
- Network: network egress is denied by default. Any egress must be explicitly allowed via an allowance list and reviewed by security.
- File system: readonly mount only; write operations redirected to ephemeral tmpfs with 5s lifetime.
- Timeouts: wallclock timeout 500ms for preprocess, 1000ms for postprocess unless a higher SLA is approved.

Safety and security controls (policy):
- Data handling: adapters must declare PII handling in manifest; access to customer prompt text must be approved and audited. No secret material (keys/tokens) may be embedded in adapter artifacts.
- Signing: vendor artifacts must be signed with an org key; runtime verifies signature and manifest hash.
- RBAC: registration requires a service account with role runtime.adapter.register. Role separation enforced by policy.

Observability and SLOs:
- Mandatory telemetry: adapters must emit structured telemetry for: invocation, error (with taxonomy), duration_ms, memory_mb, and capability_version. Telemetry follows our typed-metric schema.
- Tracing: adapters must propagate the request trace id and add a span with name adapter.<name>.execute. Span duration must capture wallclock time and recorded CPU delta.
- SLO: adapters are included in the parent request 99th percentile latency SLO. Any adapter that causes SLO breach after a canary period will be automatically disabled by the runtime fallback controller.

Testing and certification:
- Unit tests: adapters must include a test manifest and at least five deterministic unit tests covering happy path, timeout, malformed input, and capability downgrade.
- Integration tests: the adapter package must include an integration test invoking the sandboxed runtime in CI and asserting telemetry and span emission.
- Certification matrix: the Runtime Extensions team will publish a compatibility matrix. Certified versions appear in the public model catalog with an extra badge.

Safe rollout and emergency rollback playbook (operational procedure):
1) Staging registration: vendor registers adapter in staging with a limited tenant allowlist (max 3 tenants).
2) Canary traffic: route 0.5–3% of production requests for 48 hours. Monitor: latency_99p, error_rate, memory_peak.
3) Gradual expansion: double fingerprint every 48 hours if no regressions.
4) Emergency rollback: runtime has a kill switch. Operator steps:
   - Acknowledge incident in PagerDuty.
   - Disable adapter via runtime CLI: redwood runtime adapter disable <name> --env=prod
   - If unavailable, circuit the entire extension point using the fallback policy.
5) Postmortem: vendor and Runtime Extensions team conduct joint blameless postmortem within 72 hours.

CLI and example commands:
- Register: redwood runtime adapter register --manifest ./manifest.json --artifact ./adapter.wasm
- Inspect: redwood runtime adapter describe <name> --env=staging
- Disable: redwood runtime adapter disable <name> --reason \"slo-regression\"

Decision rationale and alternatives considered:
- Why WASM default? WASM provides strong sandboxing and deterministic resource controls. Sidecars are permitted when native dependencies are required, but sidecars require a stricter security review.
- Why deny egress by default? Prevents inadvertent data exfiltration; exceptions are rare and auditable.

Compatibility and upgrade policy:
- Minor manifest version changes with identical semantics are allowed to be hot swapped. Major version bumps must go through the canary rollout above.
- Runtime upgrades that change the adapter ABI require a migration shim or deprecation window of at least 90 days.

Ownership and contacts:
- Runtime Extensions (primary): runtime-extensions@redwood.example.com
- Platform Security (sec review): platform-security@redwood.example.com
- Support runbook: see /confluence/eng-platform/runbooks/adapter-fallback-runbook-2025.json

Appendix A — Manifest validation pseudo‑schema:
- name: regex ^[a-z0-9_-]+$
- version: semver 2.0
- capabilities: subset of allowed list [preprocess, postprocess, route_hint, telemetry_enricher]
- hash: sha256
- min_runtime_version, max_runtime_version: semantic tokens

Appendix B — Observability sample metric mapping (CSV):
metric_name,unit,required
adapter.invocations,count,yes
adapter.errors,count,yes
adapter.duration_ms,ms,yes
adapter.memory_mb,mb,yes

Related work and references:
- api-standards/canonical-request-schema-and-validation-playbook-2025.json
- observability-standards/telemetry-ingestion-and-retention-guidelines-2025.json
- infra-standards/airgapped-artifact-sync-and-telemetry-proxy-2025.json

- 2025-03-25: security and telemetry sections updated (Samir Patel)
- 2025-04-02: public rollout approval and small clarifications (Lena Morgan)

source.title: Dependency Fencing and Approximation Guide
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
Summary
-------
This document describes an operational pattern we call dependency fencing with approximation: proactive isolation of unhealthy or high-latency downstream services combined with controlled use of approximate responses to preserve SLOs and user experience. It provides design patterns, policy examples, runbook procedures, observability/playbook templates, and ownership guidance for platform teams that operate LLM inference and platform services.

Motivation
----------
- Many Redwood platform services depend on third-party or internal systems (e.g., feature stores, rerankers, external knowledge indexes). A single slow dependency can cascade into higher tail latency and SLO breaches.
- Full failover to a simpler model (e.g., locally cached embeddings) may improve latency but change output quality. We need predictable safety mechanisms that:
  - Minimize user-visible errors
  - Maintain latency and throughput SLOs
  - Provide measurable quality degradation and a clear remediation path

Scope and Audience
------------------
This guide targets platform engineers, oncall, and service owners for:
- Redwood serving runtime components (router, prefix cache, KV layer)
- Downstream enrichment services (rerankers, external search, personalization)
- Observability and SLO owners who need playbooks to manage approximation tradeoffs

Definitions
-----------
- Fencing: policy-driven temporary isolation of a dependency (route-level or tenant-level) that prevents the service from being called under defined conditions.
- Approximation: returning a best-effort substitute response instead of calling the fenced dependency (examples: cached result, synthetic default, degraded model variant).
- Approximation policy: configuration describing triggers, fallback type, TTL, quality budget, and metrics to track.

Design Patterns
---------------
1) Canary Fence
   - Use slow-start thresholds to fence a dependency only for a subset of traffic (e.g., 5% of requests or a single tenant) to validate approximation behavior before wider enforcement.

2) Tenant-scoped Fencing
   - Apply safe-mode per-tenant when their requests exhibit high error or latency ratio, preventing global impact.

3) Graceful Approximation Chains
   - Multi-tier fallback: (a) in-memory cache; (b) locally hosted lightweight model; (c) canned default; (d) error. Advance through tiers by severity.

4) Quality Budgeting
   - Each tenant has a daily/weekly approximation budget (max % of requests that can be approximated). When the budget is exhausted the system falls back to blocking or queuing requests.

Policy Example (YAML)
---------------------
Sample approximation policy to be applied at the routing layer or sidecar: 

```yaml
policy_name: reranker-fence-v1
triggers:
  - metric: p95_latency_ms
    window: 60s
    threshold: 450
    action: fence_candidate
  - metric: error_rate
    window: 5m
    threshold: 0.05
    action: tenant_scoped_fence
fence_strategy:
  scope: tenant
  duration: 5m
approximation:
  tiered:
    - type: local_cache
      ttl: 30m
    - type: lightweight_model
      model_variant: reranker-lite-v2
      max_tokens: 128
    - type: canned_default
maintenance_windows:
  allow_rollouts: false
observability:
  metrics:
    - name: approximations.count
    - name: approximations.by_tier
    - name: quality.delta_from_baseline
  dashboards: service/reranker/approximation-overview
```

Operational SLO Considerations
------------------------------
- Primary platform SLOs (e.g., text-generation request p95 < 300ms) must be preserved by approximation. Any policy that increases user-visible latency or error must be gated by an emergency-owner approval.
- Record both latency and quality SLOs: e.g., quality SLO: "median rerank position delta <= 2 compared to baseline on golden prompts". Approximation must report deviations.

Required Metrics and Dashboards
------------------------------
Create the following panels in the approximation dashboard for each service using fencing:
- Approximations per minute (total and by tier)
- Approximation % of total traffic (tenant and global)
- Quality delta on golden set (rolling 1h / 24h)
- Downstream p50/p95/p99 latency and error rate
- Approximation budget remaining per tenant
- Number of active fences and their scopes

Alerting Goals (suggested)
--------------------------
- P0: approximation_rate > 30% for >= 10m and quality.delta_from_baseline > 0.25 => Pager to oncall and owner_team
- P1: single tenant approximation_rate > 50% for >= 5m => Email + Slack to tenant owner
- P2: fence_count > 5 across region for >= 15m => Ops ticket and engineering review

Runbook: When a Dependency Is Fenced
------------------------------------
1) Notification: The routing system posts an automated incident to #platform-ops with scope: service, affected_tenants, trigger_metric, and suggested approximation tier.
2) Triage: Owner verifies the metric and checks the downstream health dashboard (latency histograms, recent deploys, infra alerts).
3) Decide:
   - If downstream is degraded due to capacity: scale (autoscale pools, add burst nodes) and keep fence for 3m while warming caches.
   - If downstream is degraded due to code/error: initiate a rollback or patch. Approximations remain until fix is deployed and verified.
   - If the degradation is due to a third-party outage: extend tenant-scoped fences and increase approximation tier to highest-fidelity local model.
4) Monitor quality: Run the golden prompt set against approximated responses and compare the baseline. If delta exceeds configured thresholds, escalate to the model-quality team.
5) Post-incident: create a short RCA and add required follow-ups (capacity changes, circuit improvements, SLA changes).

Sample CLI: fence a dependency for a tenant
----------------------------------------
```bash
# Fence the reranker for tenant 'acme-inc' for 10 minutes and use local_cache fallback
redwoodctl fences create --service reranker --tenant acme-inc --duration 10m --fallback local_cache --note "emergency fencing due to p95 spike"

# List active fences
redwoodctl fences list --region us-west-2

# Remove fence immediately
redwoodctl fences delete --id fence-0a1b2c
```

Quality Measurement and Golden Prompts
--------------------------------------
- Maintain a golden prompt set per critical service (stored in git: infra/approx/golden-prompts/).
- Golden set should include: 50 tenant-representative examples, edge cases, and privacy-sensitive inputs (sanitized).
- Run approximate outputs through automated evals: BLEU/ROUGE is not always sufficient for rerankers; use task-specific metrics (e.g., click-through proxy, position delta).

Safety and Governance
---------------------
- All approximation policies and fences must be auditable. Log: who created the fence, reason, policy id, scope, and automatic expiry.
- Sensitive tenants (enterprise customers with Private deployments) must have tenant-level policy overrides disabled by default and require support approval.

Testing and Rollout Guidance
---------------------------
1) Unit test: Add mocks for all approximation tiers in unit tests.
2) Integration test: Simulate downstream latency and assert approximation takes effect and quality metrics are collected.
3) Staged rollout: start with 1% of traffic in staging, then 5% in prod, then full rollout with a 24h observation window.

Backwards Compatibility and Migration
------------------------------------
- When introducing new approximation tiers, ensure old tournaments/experiments use feature flags.
- Provide a deprecation window (>= 90 days) for clients that depend on a previous fallback contract (e.g., canned-default response schema).

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Platform runtime (team: inference-runtime) owns routing/fencing implementation and dashboards.
- Service owners (e.g., reranker team) own golden prompts and quality thresholds.
- SRE owns alerting and oncall runbooks.

Postmortem Template (add to incident)
------------------------------------
- Title: Incident: <service> fenced via policy <policy_name>
- Timeline: events with timestamps
- Root cause: causal factors
- Impact: affected tenants, approximation minutes, quality delta
- Action items: owner, ETA, verification steps

Appendix: Example Dashboard Panel Definitions (Grafana style)
-----------------------------------------------------------
1) Panel: Approximation Overview
   - Query: sum(rate(approximations_total[5m])) by (tier)
   - Viz: stacked area, annotate fence events
2) Panel: Quality Delta (golden)
   - Query: avg_over_time(quality.delta_from_baseline[1h]) by (service)
   - Thresholds: warn=0.1, crit=0.25

Appendix: Example Audit Event (JSON)
-----------------------------------
```json
{"event_type":"fence.create","service":"reranker","scope":"tenant","tenant":"acme-inc","policy":"reranker-fence-v1","created_by":"ava.chen","expires_at":"2025-03-04T15:22:00Z"}
```

Related Work and Links
----------------------
- Platform traffic-shepherding strategies (internal) — see related_pages list above
- Golden prompt repository: gitlab.com/redwood/infra/approx/golden-prompts
- redwoodctl fences CLI docs: internal CLI playbook

FAQ
---
Q: Will approximation bias results for certain tenants?
A: Potentially. That is why we require tenant-scoped budgets and golden-prompt verification. Any sustained bias observed must be surfaced to product and privacy review.

Q: How long can an approximation stay in effect?
A: Default policy max is 24 hours for global fences; tenant fences default to 6 hours and may be extended by an owner. All fences must auto-expire and be re-evaluated.

Revision History
----------------
- 2024-11-12: Draft created (Ava Chen)
- 2025-01-20: Added quality budget section and policy examples (Diego Martinez)
- 2025-03-04: Finalized alerts and runbook steps; published (Priya Nair)

- Record both latency and quality SLOs: e.g., quality SLO: "median rerank position delta <= 2 compared to baseline on golden prompts". Approximation must report deviations.

Required Metrics and Dashboards
------------------------------
Create the following panels in the approximation dashboard for each service using fencing:
- Approximations per minute (total and by tier)
- Approximation % of total traffic (tenant and global)
- Quality delta on golden set (rolling 1h / 24h)
- Downstream p50/p95/p99 latency and error rate
- Approximation budget remaining per tenant
- Number of active fences and their scopes

Alerting Goals (suggested)
--------------------------
- P0: approximation_rate > 30% for >= 10m and quality.delta_from_baseline > 0.25 => Pager to oncall and owner_team
- P1: single tenant approximation_rate > 50% for >= 5m => Email + Slack to tenant owner
- P2: fence_count > 5 across region for >= 15m => Ops ticket and engineering review

Runbook: When a Dependency Is Fenced
------------------------------------
1) Notification: The routing system posts an automated incident to #platform-ops with scope: service, affected_tenants, trigger_metric, and suggested approximation tier.
2) Triage: Owner verifies the metric and checks the downstream health dashboard (latency histograms, recent deploys, infra alerts).
3) Decide:
   - If downstream is degraded due to capacity: scale (autoscale pools, add burst nodes) and keep fence for 3m while warming caches.
   - If downstream is degraded due to code/error: initiate a rollback or patch. Approximations remain until fix is deployed and verified.
   - If the degradation is due to a third-party outage: extend tenant-scoped fences and increase approximation tier to highest-fidelity local model.
4) Monitor quality: Run the golden prompt set against approximated responses and compare the baseline. If delta exceeds configured thresholds, escalate to the model-quality team.
5) Post-incident: create a short RCA and add required follow-ups (capacity changes, circuit improvements, SLA changes).

Sample CLI: fence a dependency for a tenant
----------------------------------------
```bash
# Fence the reranker for tenant 'acme-inc' for 10 minutes and use local_cache fallback
redwoodctl fences create --service reranker --tenant acme-inc --duration 10m --fallback local_cache --note "emergency fencing due to p95 spike"

# List active fences
redwoodctl fences list --region us-west-2

# Remove fence immediately
redwoodctl fences delete --id fence-0a1b2c
```

Quality Measurement and Golden Prompts
--------------------------------------
- Maintain a golden prompt set per critical service (stored in git: infra/approx/golden-prompts/).
- Golden set should include: 50 tenant-representative examples, edge cases, and privacy-sensitive inputs (sanitized).
- Run approximate outputs through automated evals: BLEU/ROUGE is not always sufficient for rerankers; use task-specific metrics (e.g., click-through proxy, position delta).

Safety and Governance
---------------------
- All approximation policies and fences must be auditable. Log: who created the fence, reason, policy id, scope, and automatic expiry.
- Sensitive tenants (enterprise customers with Private deployments) must have tenant-level policy overrides disabled by default and require support approval.

Testing and Rollout Guidance
---------------------------
1) Unit test: Add mocks for all approximation tiers in unit tests.
2) Integration test: Simulate downstream latency and assert approximation takes effect and quality metrics are collected.
3) Staged rollout: start with 1% of traffic in staging, then 5% in prod, then full rollout with a 24h observation window.

Backwards Compatibility and Migration
------------------------------------
- When introducing new approximation tiers, ensure old tournaments/experiments use feature flags.
- Provide a deprecation window (>= 90 days) for clients that depend on a previous fallback contract (e.g., canned-default response schema).

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Platform runtime (team: inference-runtime) owns routing/fencing implementation and dashboards.
- Service owners (e.g., reranker team) own golden prompts and quality thresholds.
- SRE owns alerting and oncall runbooks.

Postmortem Template (add to incident)
------------------------------------
- Title: Incident: <service> fenced via policy <policy_name>
- Timeline: events with timestamps
- Root cause: causal factors
- Impact: affected tenants, approximation minutes, quality delta
- Action items: owner, ETA, verification steps

Appendix: Example Dashboard Panel Definitions (Grafana style)
-----------------------------------------------------------
1) Panel: Approximation Overview
   - Query: sum(rate(approximations_total[5m])) by (tier)
   - Viz: stacked area, annotate fence events
2) Panel: Quality Delta (golden)
   - Query: avg_over_time(quality.delta_from_baseline[1h]) by (service)
   - Thresholds: warn=0.1, crit=0.25

Appendix: Example Audit Event (JSON)
-----------------------------------
```json
{"event_type":"fence.create","service":"reranker","scope":"tenant","tenant":"acme-inc","policy":"reranker-fence-v1","created_by":"ava.chen","expires_at":"2025-03-04T15:22:00Z"}
```

Related Work and Links
----------------------
- Platform traffic-shepherding strategies (internal) — see related_pages list above
- Golden prompt repository: gitlab.com/redwood/infra/approx/golden-prompts
- redwoodctl fences CLI docs: internal CLI playbook

FAQ
---
Q: Will approximation bias results for certain tenants?
A: Potentially. That is why we require tenant-scoped budgets and golden-prompt verification. Any sustained bias observed must be surfaced to product and privacy review.

Q: How long can an approximation stay in effect?
A: Default policy max is 24 hours for global fences; tenant fences default to 6 hours and may be extended by an owner. All fences must auto-expire and be re-evaluated.

Revision History
----------------
- 2024-11-12: Draft created (Ava Chen)
- 2025-01-20: Added quality budget section and policy examples (Diego Martinez)
- 2025-03-04: Finalized alerts and runbook steps; published (Priya Nair)
text:
Summary
-------
This document describes an operational pattern we call dependency fencing with approximation: proactive isolation of unhealthy or high-latency downstream services combined with controlled use of approximate responses to preserve SLOs and user experience. It provides design patterns, policy examples, runbook procedures, observability/playbook templates, and ownership guidance for platform teams that operate LLM inference and platform services.

Motivation
----------
- Many Redwood platform services depend on third-party or internal systems (e.g., feature stores, rerankers, external knowledge indexes). A single slow dependency can cascade into higher tail latency and SLO breaches.
- Full failover to a simpler model (e.g., locally cached embeddings) may improve latency but change output quality. We need predictable safety mechanisms that:
  - Minimize user-visible errors
  - Maintain latency and throughput SLOs
  - Provide measurable quality degradation and a clear remediation path

Scope and Audience
------------------
This guide targets platform engineers, oncall, and service owners for:
- Redwood serving runtime components (router, prefix cache, KV layer)
- Downstream enrichment services (rerankers, external search, personalization)
- Observability and SLO owners who need playbooks to manage approximation tradeoffs

Definitions
-----------
- Fencing: policy-driven temporary isolation of a dependency (route-level or tenant-level) that prevents the service from being called under defined conditions.
- Approximation: returning a best-effort substitute response instead of calling the fenced dependency (examples: cached result, synthetic default, degraded model variant).
- Approximation policy: configuration describing triggers, fallback type, TTL, quality budget, and metrics to track.

Design Patterns
---------------
1) Canary Fence
   - Use slow-start thresholds to fence a dependency only for a subset of traffic (e.g., 5% of requests or a single tenant) to validate approximation behavior before wider enforcement.

2) Tenant-scoped Fencing
   - Apply safe-mode per-tenant when their requests exhibit high error or latency ratio, preventing global impact.

3) Graceful Approximation Chains
   - Multi-tier fallback: (a) in-memory cache; (b) locally hosted lightweight model; (c) canned default; (d) error. Advance through tiers by severity.

4) Quality Budgeting
   - Each tenant has a daily/weekly approximation budget (max % of requests that can be approximated). When the budget is exhausted the system falls back to blocking or queuing requests.

Policy Example (YAML)
---------------------
Sample approximation policy to be applied at the routing layer or sidecar: 

```yaml
policy_name: reranker-fence-v1
triggers:
  - metric: p95_latency_ms
    window: 60s
    threshold: 450
    action: fence_candidate
  - metric: error_rate
    window: 5m
    threshold: 0.05
    action: tenant_scoped_fence
fence_strategy:
  scope: tenant
  duration: 5m
approximation:
  tiered:
    - type: local_cache
      ttl: 30m
    - type: lightweight_model
      model_variant: reranker-lite-v2
      max_tokens: 128
    - type: canned_default
maintenance_windows:
  allow_rollouts: false
observability:
  metrics:
    - name: approximations.count
    - name: approximations.by_tier
    - name: quality.delta_from_baseline
  dashboards: service/reranker/approximation-overview
```

Operational SLO Considerations
------------------------------
- Primary platform SLOs (e.g., text-generation request p95 < 300ms) must be preserved by approximation. Any policy that increases user-visible latency or error must be gated by an emergency-owner approval.
- Record both latency and quality SLOs: e.g., quality SLO: "median rerank position delta <= 2 compared to baseline on golden prompts". Approximation must report deviations.

Required Metrics and Dashboards
------------------------------
Create the following panels in the approximation dashboard for each service using fencing:
- Approximations per minute (total and by tier)
- Approximation % of total traffic (tenant and global)
- Quality delta on golden set (rolling 1h / 24h)
- Downstream p50/p95/p99 latency and error rate
- Approximation budget remaining per tenant
- Number of active fences and their scopes

Alerting Goals (suggested)
--------------------------
- P0: approximation_rate > 30% for >= 10m and quality.delta_from_baseline > 0.25 => Pager to oncall and owner_team
- P1: single tenant approximation_rate > 50% for >= 5m => Email + Slack to tenant owner
- P2: fence_count > 5 across region for >= 15m => Ops ticket and engineering review

Runbook: When a Dependency Is Fenced
------------------------------------
1) Notification: The routing system posts an automated incident to #platform-ops with scope: service, affected_tenants, trigger_metric, and suggested approximation tier.
2) Triage: Owner verifies the metric and checks the downstream health dashboard (latency histograms, recent deploys, infra alerts).
3) Decide:
   - If downstream is degraded due to capacity: scale (autoscale pools, add burst nodes) and keep fence for 3m while warming caches.
   - If downstream is degraded due to code/error: initiate a rollback or patch. Approximations remain until fix is deployed and verified.
   - If the degradation is due to a third-party outage: extend tenant-scoped fences and increase approximation tier to highest-fidelity local model.
4) Monitor quality: Run the golden prompt set against approximated responses and compare the baseline. If delta exceeds configured thresholds, escalate to the model-quality team.
5) Post-incident: create a short RCA and add required follow-ups (capacity changes, circuit improvements, SLA changes).

Sample CLI: fence a dependency for a tenant
----------------------------------------
```bash
# Fence the reranker for tenant 'acme-inc' for 10 minutes and use local_cache fallback
redwoodctl fences create --service reranker --tenant acme-inc --duration 10m --fallback local_cache --note "emergency fencing due to p95 spike"

# List active fences
redwoodctl fences list --region us-west-2

# Remove fence immediately
redwoodctl fences delete --id fence-0a1b2c

Quality Measurement and Golden Prompts
--------------------------------------
- Maintain a golden prompt set per critical service (stored in git: infra/approx/golden-prompts/).
- Golden set should include: 50 tenant-representative examples, edge cases, and privacy-sensitive inputs (sanitized).
- Run approximate outputs through automated evals: BLEU/ROUGE is not always sufficient for rerankers; use task-specific metrics (e.g., click-through proxy, position delta).

Safety and Governance
---------------------

- Sensitive tenants (enterprise customers with Private deployments) must have tenant-level policy overrides disabled by default and require support approval.

Testing and Rollout Guidance
---------------------------
1) Unit test: Add mocks for all approximation tiers in unit tests.
2) Integration test: Simulate downstream latency and assert approximation takes effect and quality metrics are collected.
3) Staged rollout: start with 1% of traffic in staging, then 5% in prod, then full rollout with a 24h observation window.

Backwards Compatibility and Migration
------------------------------------
- When introducing new approximation tiers, ensure old tournaments/experiments use feature flags.
- Provide a deprecation window (>= 90 days) for clients that depend on a previous fallback contract (e.g., canned-default response schema).

Ownership and Contacts
----------------------
- Platform runtime (team: inference-runtime) owns routing/fencing implementation and dashboards.
- Service owners (e.g., reranker team) own golden prompts and quality thresholds.
- SRE owns alerting and oncall runbooks.

Postmortem Template (add to incident)
------------------------------------
- Title: Incident: <service> fenced via policy <policy_name>
- Timeline: events with timestamps
- Root cause: causal factors
- Impact: affected tenants, approximation minutes, quality delta
- Action items: owner, ETA, verification steps

Appendix: Example Dashboard Panel Definitions (Grafana style)
-----------------------------------------------------------
1) Panel: Approximation Overview
   - Query: sum(rate(approximations_total[5m])) by (tier)
   - Viz: stacked area, annotate fence events
2) Panel: Quality Delta (golden)
   - Query: avg_over_time(quality.delta_from_baseline[1h]) by (service)
   - Thresholds: warn=0.1, crit=0.25

Appendix: Example Audit Event (JSON)
-----------------------------------

{"event_type":"fence.create","service":"reranker","scope":"tenant","tenant":"acme-inc","policy":"reranker-fence-v1","created_by":"ava.chen","expires_at":"2025-03-04T15:22:00Z"}
```

Related Work and Links
----------------------

- Golden prompt repository: gitlab.com/redwood/infra/approx/golden-prompts
- redwoodctl fences CLI docs: internal CLI playbook

FAQ
---
Q: Will approximation bias results for certain tenants?
A: Potentially. That is why we require tenant-scoped budgets and golden-prompt verification. Any sustained bias observed must be surfaced to product and privacy review.

Q: How long can an approximation stay in effect?
A: Default policy max is 24 hours for global fences; tenant fences default to 6 hours and may be extended by an owner. All fences must auto-expire and be re-evaluated.

source.title: Postmortem: Hosted API latency spike and model fallback activation (2025-02-11)
source.author: Ava Chen
source.content:
h2. Summary
On 2025-02-11, the Hosted API experienced elevated p95 latency on /v1/chat/completions for ~23 minutes. Mitigation included activating an automatic model fallback policy to keep requests flowing while we reduced load on the primary model pool.

h2. Impact
* Customer-facing: increased latency, intermittent timeouts, elevated 5xx for some bursts.
* Affected product: Redwood API (Hosted).
* Affected tiers: primarily Free and Growth tiers on the default route.

h2. Timeline (UTC)
* 17:06: Alert fired: Chat latency p95 > 4.0s (threshold 2.0s) for 5 min.
* 17:11: On-call confirmed queueing on primary model pool (rw-llama-3.1-70b-instruct).
* 17:14: Mitigation: enabled *automatic model fallback* on the router policy for the default route.
* 17:16: Router began automatically rerouting overflow traffic to rw-llama-3.1-8b-instruct (fallback target) when saturation exceeded 85%.
* 17:22: Latency recovered; errors returned to baseline.
* 17:29: Fallback policy left on for 30 additional minutes to protect SLO.

h2. What we changed (Mitigation)
We activated a policy-based *auto-fallback* on the router:
* Primary: rw-llama-3.1-70b-instruct
* Fallback: rw-llama-3.1-8b-instruct
* Trigger: saturation > 85% OR queue wait > 250ms
* Scope: /v1/chat/completions, tier in {free, growth}

h2. Root cause
A mis-sized continuous batching configuration on the primary pool caused queueing to grow quickly under a bursty prompt distribution (longer-than-expected context lengths).

h2. Detection
Automated latency and queue-wait alerts fired correctly, but we lacked a dedicated alert for “fallback activated” to help confirm mitigation status.

h2. Action items
1) Add a first-class router metric + alert: fallback_activated_count by route/model. Owner: Eng Platform.
2) Update the on-call runbook to include the fallback policy toggle and rollback conditions. Owner: Eng SRE.
3) Re-tune batching defaults for rw-llama-3.1-70b-instruct on A100 pool. Owner: Serving Runtime.

h2. References
* Related page: Router policies and safe-mode toggles (internal)
* Incident ID: INC-2025-0211
